1.3: Greater in power . Allusion to his birthright.
1.7: The elders , etc. (Vulg. senes, majores natu ) ; that is to say the high-ranking officers of the Pharaoh's court, and the leading figures, the principal people of Egypt.
1.8: The first part of this prophecy relates to the tribe of Judah, but the last part to Jesus Christ, who descended from Judah according to the flesh. Your brothers will praise you ; Judah means to rent out . Jacob takes the name Judah, as he will do with most of his brothers, as a kind of omen of his future destiny.
1.10: The Atad area , whose location is unknown, was east of the Jordan according to some, west according to others.
1.16: . In the double cave , in Makpelah. See Genesis , note
1.26: Your father's blessings , etc.; that is to say, to the blessings I give you will be added the blessings I received from my fathers. Others translate from the Hebrew: Your father's blessings surpass Or will surpass the blessings of his fathers . The Vulgate, like the Hebrew text, uses the preterite tense instead of the future tense because, in prophetic predictions and promises, the things that are predicted and promised are considered to have already been accomplished. Nazarene ; that's to say separated, distant from his father, from his house; according to others, dedicated to God; according to others, crown in the Pharaoh's house. In the court of the kings of the East, the first officer is called nazir , or an officer of the crown.
1.27: In the evening, he will divide the spoils. . Although the tribe of Benjamin was one of the smallest, it was nevertheless among the strongest, because it controlled the mountain passes that led from its territory into the adjacent plains. Saint Paul, who won over so many nations to the Church, was a Benjamite.

1.17: A cerastus . The cerastes is a horned serpent, earth-colored, that hides in ruts, so that it can easily bite passersby. The meaning is that Dan will compensate for his lack of strength with cunning. Indeed, it was by surprise that he seized Laish, see Judges ,
1.15: Issachar, satisfied with the wealth of his territory which included part of the fertile plain of Esdraelon, made himself tributary to foreigners so as not to disturb his peace.
1.45: . — This prophecy was fulfilled in the time of Jesus Christ, who appeared when the Jews had just lost their sovereign authority, and who proved by his miracles that he was the Messiah whom God was to send and whom the nations had awaited.
1.9: Their pilgrimage . He called his wandering life on earth a pilgrimage, because he had a sense of his homeland beyond. Those who speak in this way, says Saint Paul, indicate that they are seeking their homeland, for if they had thought only of the one they had left, they certainly had time to return to it, but they desired a better one, that is to say, a heavenly one. See Hebrews ,

2.1: Thus were completed the heavens and the earth and all their array.
2.2: And God had finished on the seventh day his work that he had done, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work that he had done.
2.3: And God blessed the seventh day and made it holy, because on it he rested from all the work of creation that he had done.
2.4: This is the account of heaven and earth when they were created, when the Lord God made the earth and the heavens.
2.5: There was not yet on the earth any shrub of the field, and no plant of the field had yet sprouted, for the Lord God had not caused it to rain on the earth and there was no man to till the ground.
2.6: But a vapor rose from the earth and watered the entire surface of the ground.
2.7: The Lord God formed man from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and man became a living being.
2.8: Then the Lord God planted a garden in Eden in the east, and there he put the man he had formed.
2.9: And the Lord God made all kinds of trees grow out of the ground—trees that were pleasing to the eye and good for food. In the middle of the garden were the tree of life and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
2.10: A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and from there it divided into four branches.
2.11: The name of the first is Pishon; he is the one who surrounds the whole land of Hevilath, where there is gold.
2.12: And the gold in that country is good, there Also bdellium and onyx stone are found there.
2.13: The name of the second river is Gehon; it is the one that surrounds the whole land of Cush.
2.14: The name of the third is the Tigris, the one that flows east of Assur. The fourth river is the Euphrates.
2.15: The Lord God took the man and placed him in the garden of Eden to cultivate it and to take care of it.
2.16: And the Lord God commanded the man, “You may eat from every tree in the garden,
2.17: But you shall not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, for in the day that you eat from it you shall certainly die.
2.18: The Lord God said: It is not good for the man to be alone; I will make a helper suitable for him.
2.19: And the Lord God, who had formed out of the ground all the beasts of the field and all the birds of the air, brought them to man to see what he would name them; and whatever the man called each living creature.
2.20: And man gave names to all the domestic animals, the birds of the air, and all the beasts of the field, but he did not find for man a helper comparable to him.
2.21: Then the Lord God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man, and he slept; and he took one of his ribs and closed up the flesh in its place.
2.22: From the rib that he had taken out of the man, the Lord God formed a woman, and he brought her to the man.
2.23: And the man said, “This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh; she shall be called Woman, for she was taken out of Man.”.
2.24: That is why a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and they will become one flesh.
2.25: They were both naked, the man and his wife, without any shame.

3.1: Now the serpent was more crafty than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made. He said to the woman, “Did God really say, ‘You must not eat from any tree in the garden’?”
3.2: The woman answered the serpent, “We eat fruit from the trees in the garden.”.
3.3: But of the fruit of the tree which is in the middle of the garden, God said: You shall not eat of it, nor shall you touch it, lest you die.
3.4: The snake said to the woman: No, you will not die
3.5: But God knows that when you eat of it your eyes will be opened and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.
3.6: The woman saw that the fruit The fruit of the tree was good for food, pleasing to the eye, and desirable for gaining wisdom; she took some of its fruit and ate it, and she also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate it.
3.7: Then the eyes of both of them were opened and they realized they were naked; so they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.
3.8: Then they heard the sound of the Lord God passing in the garden in the cool of the day, and the man and his wife hid from the presence of the Lord God among the trees of the garden.
3.9: But the Lord God called to the man and said to him: Where are you?
3.10: He replied: I heard your voice in the garden, and I was afraid, for I was naked, and I hid .
3.11: And the Lord God said: Who told you that you are naked? Did you eat from the tree that I forbade you to eat from?
3.12: The man replied: The woman you put with me has borne me fruit from the tree and I ate some of it .
3.13: The Lord God said to the woman: Why did you do that? The woman replied: The serpent deceived me, and I ate it. .
3.14: The Lord God said to the serpent: Because you have done this, you are cursed more than all cattle and all beasts of the field; you shall go on your belly, and you shall eat dust all the days of your life. .
3.15: And I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he will strike your head, and you will strike his heel .
3.16: To the woman he said: I will multiply your sufferings, and especially those of During your pregnancy, you will give birth to sons in pain; your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you. .
3.17: He said to the man: Because you listened to your wife and ate from the tree about which I commanded you, “You must not eat from it,” because the ground is cursed because of you; through painful toil you will eat food from it all the days of your life.
3.18: It will produce thorns and thistles for you, and you will eat the plants of the field. .
3.19: By the sweat of your brow you will eat your food until you return to the ground, since from it you were taken; for dust you are and to dust you will return. .
3.20: Adam named his wife Eve, because she was the mother of all the living.
3.21: The Lord God made tunics of skin for Adam and his wife, and the in donned.
3.22: And the Lord God said: The man has now become like one of us, knowing good and evil. He must not reach out his hand and take also from the tree of life to eat and live forever. .
3.23: And the Lord God brought him out of the garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken.
3.24: And he drove out the man, and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden the cherubim and the flaming sword which turned every way to guard the way to the tree of life.

4.1: Adam knew Eve, his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to Cain, and she said, “I have acquired a man with the rescue of Lord.
4.2: She also gave birth to Abel, his brother. Abel was a shepherd of sheep, and Cain was a plowman.
4.3: After some time, Cain offered some of the fruits of the earth as an offering to the Lord,
4.4: Abel, for his part, offered some of the firstborn of his flock and their fat portions. The Lord looked upon Abel and his offering.
4.5: But he did not look at Cain and his offering. Cain was very angry, and his face fell.
4.6: The Lord said to Cain: Why are you angry and why is your face downcast?
4.7: If you do what is right, will you not be accepted? And if you do not do what is right, does not sin crouch at your door? Its desire is for you, but you must rule over it. .
4.8: Cain said to Abel, his brother: Let's go to the fields . And, as they were in the fields, Cain rose up against Abel, his brother, and killed him.
4.9: And the Lord said to Cain: Where is Abel, your brother? He replied: I don't know, am I my brother's keeper?
4.10: The Lord said: What did you do? The voice of your brother's blood cries out from the earth to me .
4.11: Now you are cursed from the ground, which opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand.
4.12: When you cultivate the land, it will no longer yield its fruits; you will be a restless wanderer on the land. .
4.13: Cain said to the Lord: My grief is too great for me to bear.
4.14: Behold, you are driving me today from this land, and I will be hidden from your sight; I will be a wanderer and a fugitive on the earth, and whoever finds me will kill me. .
4.15: The Lord said to him: Well, if anyone kills Cain, Cain will be avenged seven times over. . And the Lord put a mark on Cain so that no one who met him would kill him.
4.16: Then Cain went away from the presence of the Lord and lived in the land of Nod, east of Eden.
4.17: Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to Enoch. And he began to build a city, which he called Enoch, after his son.
4.18: Irad was born to Enoch and he fathered Maviael, Maviael fathered Methusel, and Methusel fathered Lamech.
4.19: Lamech took two wives, the name of one was Ada, and that of the second was Sella.
4.20: Ada gave birth to Jabel: he was the father of those who dwell in tents and among flocks.
4.21: His brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all those who play the harp and the flute.
4.22: Sella, for her part, gave birth to Tubal-Cain, who forged all kinds of cutting tools of bronze and iron. Tubal-Cain's sister was Naamah.
4.23: Lamech said to his wives: Ada and Sella, hear my voice, women of Lamech, listen to my words: I killed a man for my injury and a young man for my bruise.
4.24: Cain will be avenged seven times and Lamech seventy-seven times.
4.25: Adam knew his wife again, and she gave birth to a son and named him Seth, for, she said, God has given me descendants in place of Abel, whom Cain killed. .
4.26: Seth also had a son, whom he named Enos. It was then that people began to call upon the name of the Lord.

5.1: This is the book of Adam's story. When God created Adam, he made him in God's image.
5.2: He created them male and female, and he blessed them, and he named them Man when they were created.
5.3: Adam lived one hundred and thirty years and he fathered a son in his own likeness, after his image, and he named him Seth.
5.4: Adam lived eight hundred years after he fathered Seth, and he had other sons and daughters.
5.5: The whole time Adam lived was nine hundred and thirty years, and he died.
5.6: Seth lived one hundred and five years, and he fathered Aenos.
5.7: After he fathered Enos, Seth lived eight hundred and seven years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.8: All the time that Seth lived was nine hundred and twelve years, and he died.
5.9: Enos lived for ninety years and fathered Cainan.
5.10: After he fathered Cainan, Enos lived eight hundred and fifteen years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.11: The whole time that Enos lived was nine hundred and five years, and he died.
5.12: Cainan lived seventy years, and he fathered Malaleel.
5.13: After he fathered Malaleel, Cainan lived eight hundred and forty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.14: All the time that Cainan lived was nine hundred and ten years, and he died.
5.15: Malaleel lived sixty-five years, and he fathered Jared.
5.16: After he fathered Jared, Malaleel lived eight hundred and thirty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.17: The whole time that Malaleel lived was eight hundred and ninety-five years, and he died.
5.18: Jared lived one hundred and sixty-two years, and he fathered Enoch.
5.19: After he fathered Enoch, Jared lived eight hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.20: Jared lived for nine hundred and sixty-two years, and then he died.
5.21: Enoch lived sixty-five years, and he fathered Methuselah.
5.22: After he fathered Methuselah, Enoch walked with God for three hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.23: The entire time that Enoch lived was three hundred and sixty-five years.
5.24: Enoch walked with God, and was not seen again, for God had taken him.
5.25: Methuselah lived one hundred and eighty-seven years, and he fathered Lamech.
5.26: After he fathered Lamech, Methuselah lived seven hundred and eighty-two years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
5.27: The whole time that Methuselah lived was nine hundred and sixty-nine years, and he died.
5.28: Lamech lived one hundred and eighty-two years and fathered a son.
5.29: He named him Noah, saying: This one will relieve us of our weariness and of the painful toil of our hands, from this ground which the Lord has cursed.
5.30: After he fathered Noah, Lamech lived
5.31: The entire time that Lamech lived was seven hundred and seventy-seven years, and he died.
5.32: Noah, at the age of five hundred years, fathered Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

6.1: When men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born to them,
6.2: The sons of God saw that the daughters of men were beautiful, and they took wives for themselves from among all those who pleased them.
6.3: And the Lord said: My spirit shall not dwell in man forever, for man is but flesh, and his days shall be one hundred and twenty years.
6.4: Now the giants were on the earth in those days, and this afterward, when the sons of God came to the daughters of men, and they bore children to them: they were the heroes of old.
6.5: The Lord saw that the wickedness of men was great in the earth, and that every inclination of their hearts was only evil all the time.
6.6: And the Lord regretted that he had made man on the earth, and he was grieved in his heart,
6.7: And he said, “I will wipe from the face of the earth the human race I have created—and with them the livestock, the reptiles and the birds of the air—for I regret that I have made them.”.
6.8: But Noah found favor in the eyes of the Lord.
6.9: This is the story of Noah. Noah was a righteous man, upright among the men of his time; Noah walked with God.
6.10: Noah fathered three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
6.11: But the earth became corrupt in God's sight and was filled with violence.
6.12: God looked at the earth, and behold, it was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted its way on the earth.
6.13: Then God said to Noah, “The end of all flesh has come before me, for the earth is full of violence because of them; I am going to destroy them and the earth.”.
6.14: Make yourself an ark of resinous wood, make it composed of cells and coat it with bitumen inside and out.
6.15: This is how you shall make it: the length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, its width fifty cubits and its height thirty.
6.16: You shall make an opening in the ark, and make a cubit from the roof to it; you shall set up a door in the side of the ark, and you shall make a first, a second and a third story of cells.
6.17: And I will bring a flood, an inundation of the earth, to destroy from under heaven all flesh which has the breath of life in it; everything that is on the earth shall perish.
6.18: But I will establish my covenant with you, and you will enter the ark, you and your sons, your wife and your sons' wives with you.
6.19: Of all living things, of all flesh, you shall bring into the ark two of every kind, to keep them alive with you; they shall be male and female.
6.20: Birds of various kinds, domestic animals of various kinds, and every kind of animal that creeps on the ground, two of every kind will come to you, so that you may preserve their lives.
6.21: And you, take some of all the food that is eaten and store it up with you, so that it may be food for you and for them.
6.22: Noah set to work; he did everything that God had commanded him.

7.1: The Lord said to Noah, “Go into the ark, you and all your household, for I have seen that you are righteous before me in the midst of this generation.”.
7.2: Of all the clean animals, you shall take with you seven pairs, males and their females, and of all the animals that are not clean, you shall take two, a male and its female.
7.3: Seven pairs of birds of the sky, males and their females, to keep alive their race on the face of the whole earth.
7.4: For in seven days I will send rain on the earth for forty days and forty nights, and I will wipe from the face of the earth every living thing that I have made.
7.5: Noah did everything the Lord had commanded him.
7.6: He was six hundred years old when the flood occurred, an inundation of the earth.
7.7: Noah entered the ark with his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives to escape the floodwaters.
7.8: Of clean animals and of those that are not clean, of birds and of everything that creeps on the ground,
7.9: Each pair, male and female, came to Noah in the ark, as God had commanded Noah.
7.10: And after seven days, the floodwaters spread over the earth.
7.11: In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month, on that day all the fountains of the great deep burst forth and the floodgates of heaven were opened
7.12: And the rain fell on the earth for forty days and forty nights.
7.13: That same day Noah entered the ark, along with Shem, Ham, and Japheth, Noah's sons, Noah's wife, and his three sons' wives with them.,
7.14: them and all beasts of various kinds, all domestic animals of various kinds, all reptiles of various kinds that crawl on the earth, and all birds of various kinds, all small birds, everything that has wings.
7.15: They came to Noah in the ark, two by two, of all flesh which had the breath of life.
7.16: They came male and female, of every kind of flesh, as God had commanded Noah. And the Lord shut the door behind him.
7.17: The flood was forty days on the earth, the waters rose and lifted the ark, and it rose above the earth.
7.18: The waters rose and became exceedingly high on the earth, and the ark floated on the waters.
7.19: The waters, having grown larger and larger, covered all the high mountains that are under the entire sky.
7.20: The waters rose fifteen cubits above the mountains they covered.
7.21: All flesh that moves on the earth perishes: birds, domestic animals, wild beasts, everything that creeps on the earth, as well as all men.
7.22: Of everything that existed on the dry earth, everything that had the breath of life in its nostrils died.
7.23: Every living thing that was on the face of the earth was destroyed, from man to domestic animal, to reptiles and to birds of the air; they were exterminated from the earth, and only Noah and those who were with him in the ark remained.
7.24: The waters were high on the earth for one hundred and fifty days.

8.1: God remembered Noah and all the wild animals and domesticated animals that were with him in the ark, and God caused a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters receded,
8.2: The springs of the deep and the floodgates of heaven were closed, and the rain ceased to fall from the sky.
8.3: The waters receded from the earth, going back and forth, and at the end of one hundred and fifty days they subsided.
8.4: In the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat.
8.5: The waters continued to recede until the tenth month, and in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the mountain peaks appeared.
8.6: After forty days, Noah opened the window he had made in the ark,
8.7: and released the raven, which went out, going back and forth, until the waters were dried up from above the earth.
8.8: He let go Next the dove from beside him, to see if the waters had receded from the surface of the earth.
8.9: But the dove, finding nowhere to place the sole of its foot, returned to him in the ark, because there was Again waters covered the face of the whole earth. He reached out his hand and, taking it, brought it back to him into the ark.
8.10: He waited another seven days, and again he released the dove from the ark,
8.11: And the dove returned to him in the evening, and behold, a freshly plucked olive leaf was in its beak, and Noah recognized that the waters no longer covered the earth.
8.12: He waited another seven days, and he released the dove, and it did not return to him.
8.13: In the year six hundred and one, in the first month, on the first day of the month, the waters had dried up from the earth. Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked, and behold, the surface of the ground was dry.
8.14: In the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the land was dry.
8.15: Then God spoke to Noah, saying:
8.16: Get out of the ark, you and your wife, your sons and your sons' wives with you.
8.17: Bring out with you every kind of animal that is with you: birds, domestic animals, and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth, so that they may be abundant on the earth and be fruitful and multiply on the earth.
8.18: Noah went out, he and his sons, his wife and his sons' wives.
8.19: All the beasts, all the reptiles and all the birds, all the creatures that move on the earth, according to their kinds, came out of the ark.
8.20: Noah built an altar to the Lord and, having taken some of all the clean animals and of all the clean birds, he offered burnt offerings on the altar.
8.21: The Lord smelled the pleasing aroma, and the Lord said in his heart, “Never again will I curse the ground because of humans, for the inclination of their hearts is evil from their youth; and never again will I destroy every living creature, as I have done.”.
8.22: From now on, as long as the earth endures, sowing and reaping, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night will not cease.

9.1: God blessed Noah and his sons and said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth.”.
9.2: You will be feared and dreaded by every beast of the earth, every bird of the sky, every creature that creeps on the earth, and all the fish of the sea: they are given into your hands.
9.3: Everything that moves and lives will serve as food for you; I give you all of this, as I had given you green grass.
9.4: However, you shall not eat flesh with its soul., that's to say with his blood.
9.5: And for your blood, I will demand an accounting for your souls; I will demand an accounting from every beast, by the hand of man, by the hand of man who is his brother, I will demand again the soul of the man.
9.6: Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed, for in the image of God has God made humankind.
9.7: As for you, be fruitful and multiply, and spread out upon the earth and multiply there.
9.8: God said Again to Noah and his sons with him:
9.9: And I will establish my covenant with you and with your descendants after you,
9.10: with every living creature that is with you, birds, domestic animals, and all the beasts of the earth with you, from all those that came out of the ark to every beast of the earth.
9.11: I establish my covenant with you: no more flesh will be destroyed by the waters of a flood, and there will never again be a flood to ravage the earth.
9.12: And God said, “This is the sign of the covenant I am setting between me and you and every living creature with you, for all generations to come.”.
9.13: I have placed my bow in the clouds, and it will become a sign of the covenant between me and the earth.
9.14: When I gather clouds over the earth, the rainbow will appear in the clouds,
9.15: And I will remember my covenant between me and you and every living creature of all flesh, and the waters will no longer become a flood destroying all flesh.
9.16: The bow will be in the clouds, and as I gaze upon it, I will remember the eternal covenant that exists between God and all living beings of every kind that are on the earth.
9.17: And God said to Noah, “This is the sign of the covenant I have established between me and all flesh that is on the earth.”.
9.18: The sons of Noah who came out of the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and Ham was the father of Canaan.
9.19: These three are the sons of Noah, and it was through them that the whole earth was populated.
9.20: Noah, who was a farmer, began to plant a vineyard.
9.21: Having drunk wine, he became drunk, and he uncovered himself in the middle of his tent.
9.22: Ham, the father of Canaan, saw his father's nakedness, and he went outside and told his two brothers.
9.23: Then Shem and Japheth took the cloak of Noah And, having put him on their shoulders, they walked backwards and covered their father's nakedness.
9.24: Because their faces were turned away, they did not see their father's nakedness. When Noah awoke from his drunkenness, he learned what his youngest son had done to him.,
9.25: And he said, “Cursed be Canaan! He shall be to his brothers the servant of servants.”
9.26: Then he said: Blessed be the Lord, the God of Shem, and may Canaan be his servant.
9.27: May God give Japheth space, may he dwell in the tents of Shem, and may Canaan be his servant.
9.28: Noah lived for three hundred and fifty years after the flood.
9.29: All the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years, and he died.

10.1: This is the account of the descendants of Noah's sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Sons were born to them after the flood.
10.2: Sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Thubal, Mosoch and Tiras.
10.3: Sons of Gomer: Ascenez, Riphath and Thogorma.
10.4: Sons of Javan: Elisa and Tharsis, Cetthim and Dodanim.
10.5: From them came the peoples scattered throughout the islands of the nations, in their miscellaneous countries, each according to their language, according to their families, according to their nations.
10.6: Sons of Ham: Cush, Mizraim, Phut and Canaan.
10.7: Sons of Chus: Saba, Hevilah, Sabbatha, Regma and Sabbathaca. Sons of Regma: Saba and Dadan.
10.8: Chus fathered Nimrod: he was the first powerful man on earth.
10.9: He was a valiant hunter before the Lord, that is why it is said: Like Nimrod, a valiant hunter before the Lord.
10.10: The beginning of his empire was Babel, Arach, Ahad and Chalanne in the land of Sennacherib.
10.11: From that country he went to Assyria, and built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Chalé
10.12: and Resen, between Nineveh and Chalé, is the large city.
10.13: Mesraim fathered the Ludim, the Anamim, the Laabim, the Nephthuim,
10.14: the Phetrusim, the Chasluim, from whom came the Philistines and the Caphtorim.
10.15: Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn and Heth,
10.16: as well as the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Gergesites,
10.17: the Hivites, the Aracaeans, the Sinaeans,
10.18: the Aradians, the Samaria, and the Hamathites. Then the families of the Canaanites spread out in the country,
10.19: and the territory of the Canaanites went from Sidon, in the direction of Gerar, as far as Gaza and, in the direction of Sodom, Gomorrah, Adamah, and Zeboiim, as far as Laza.
10.20: These are the sons of Ham, according to their families, their languages, and their miscellaneous countries, within their nations.
10.21: Sons were also born to Shem, who is the father of all the sons of Eber and the elder brother of Japheth.
10.22: Sons of Shem: Elam, Assur, Arphaxad, Lud and Aram.
10.23: Sons of Aram: Us, Hul, Gether and Mes.
10.24: Arphaxad begat Salé, and Salé begat Heber.
10.25: And two sons were born to Eber: the name of the one was Peleg, because in his days the earth was divided, and the name of his brother was Jectan.
10.26: Jectan begat Elmodad, Saleph, Asarmoth, Jarah,
10.29: Ophir, Hevilah, and Jobab. All these are sons of Jectan.
10.30: The country they inhabited was the eastern mountain, from Mesha towards Sephar.
10.31: These are the sons of Shem, according to their families, according to their languages, in their various countries, according to their nations.
10.32: These are the families of Noah's sons according to their generations, in their nations. It is from them that have come out nations Who spread across the earth after the flood.

11.1: The whole earth had one language and the same words.
11.2: Having come from the East, men They found a plain in the land of Sennaar, and they settled there.
11.3: They said to one another, “Come, let us make bricks and bake them thoroughly.” And they used bricks instead of stones, and bitumen instead of cement.
11.4: They also said, “Come, let us build ourselves a city and a tower with its top in the heavens, and let us make a memorial for ourselves, lest we be scattered over the face of the whole earth.”.
11.5: But the Lord came down to see the city and the tower that the sons of men were building.
11.6: And the Lord said: Behold, they are one people and they all have one language, and this work is the beginning of their undertakings; now nothing will hinder them from accomplishing their plans.
11.7: Come on, let's go down, and there even Let's confuse their language, so that they can no longer understand each other's language.
11.8: This is how the Lord He scattered them from there over the face of all the earth, and they stopped building the city.
11.9: That is why it was called Babel, because there the Lord confused the language of the whole earth, and from there the Lord scattered them over the face of the whole earth.
11.10: This is the story of Shem: Shem, aged one hundred years, fathered Arphaxad, two years after the flood.
11.11: After he fathered Arphaxad, Shem lived five hundred years and fathered sons and daughters.
11.12: Arphaxad lived thirty-five years, and he fathered Salé.
11.13: After he fathered Salé, Arphaxad lived four hundred and three years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
11.14: Salé lived for thirty years, and he fathered Héber.
11.15: After he fathered Eber, Salé lived
11.16: Heber lived thirty-four years, and he fathered Peleg.
11.17: After he fathered Peleg, Heber lived four hundred and thirty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
11.18: Phaleg lived for thirty years, and he fathered Reü.
11.19: After he fathered Reu, Peleg lived two hundred and nine years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
11.20: Reü lived thirty-two years, and he fathered Sarug.
11.21: After he fathered Sarug, Reü lived two hundred and seven years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
11.22: Sarug lived for thirty years, and he fathered Nachor.
11.23: After he fathered Nahor, Sarug lived two hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
11.24: Nahor lived twenty-nine years, and he fathered Terah.
11.25: After he fathered Terah, Nahor lived
11.119: years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
11.26: Terah lived seventy years, and he fathered Abram, Nahor, and Aran.
11.27: This is the story of Terah. Terah fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran. Haran fathered Lot.
11.28: And Aran died in the presence of Terah, his father, in the land of his birth, in Ur of the Chaldeans.
11.29: Abram and Nahor took wives: the name of Abram's wife was Sarai, and the name of Nahor's wife was Melcha, daughter of Aran, father of Melcha and father of Jesca.
11.30: But Sarai was barren: she had no children.
11.31: Terah took Abram his son, and Lot the son of Haran, his grandson, and Sarai his daughter-in-law, wife of Abram his son, and they went out together from Ur of the Chaldeans to go to the land of Canaan, but when they came to Haran, they settled there.
11.32: The days of Terah were two hundred and five years, and Terah died in Haran.

12.1: The Lord said to Abram, “Go from your country, your people and your father’s household to the land I will show you.”.
12.2: I will make you into a great nation, I will bless you, and I will make your name great. You will be a blessing:
12.3: I will bless those who bless you, and whoever curses you, I will curse him, and all the families of the earth will be blessed through you.
12.4: Abram went, as the Lord had told him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five years old when he left Haran.
12.5: Abram took Sarai, his wife, and Lot, his brother's son, along with all their possessions and the servants they had acquired in Haran, and they set out for the land of Canaan. And they arrived in the land of Canaan.
12.6: Abram traveled through the land to the place called Shechem, to the oak of Moreh. The Canaanites were then in the land.
12.7: The Lord appeared to Abram and him He said, “I will give this land to your descendants.” And Abram built an altar there to the Lord who had appeared to him.
12.8: From there he went on to the mountain east of Bethel, and pitched his tent, with Bethel to the west and Ai to the east. Again He built an altar to the Lord, and he called on the name of the Lord.
12.9: Then Abram moved on, from encampment to encampment, towards the South.
12.10: There was a famine in the land, and Abram went down to Egypt to stay there, for the famine was severe in the land.
12.11: As he was about to enter Egypt, he said to Sarai, his wife, “Look, I know that you are a beautiful woman,
12.12: When the Egyptians see you, they will say: This is his wife, and they will kill me and let you live.
12.13: Say that you are my sister, so that I may be treated well for your sake, and that my life may be spared out of respect for you.
12.14: When Abram arrived in Egypt, the Egyptians saw that his wife was very beautiful.
12.15: Pharaoh's officials, having seen her, praised her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken. and taken away in Pharaoh's house.
12.16: He treated Abram well for her sake, and Abram received sheep, oxen, donkeys, male and female servants, female donkeys, and camels.
12.17: But the Lord struck Pharaoh and his household with great plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife.
12.18: Pharaoh then summoned Abram and him He said: What have you done to me? Why didn't you tell me she was your wife?
12.19: Why did you say, "She is my sister," so I took her as my wife? Now, here is your wife; take her and go.
12.20: And Pharaoh, having given orders to his people concerning Abram, they sent him away, along with his wife and all that belonged to him.

13.1: Abram went up from Egypt to the South, he, his wife and all that belonged to him, and Lot with him.
13.2: Now Abram was very rich in livestock, silver, and gold.
13.3: Then he went from encampment to encampment from the South to Bethel, to the place where he had first pitched his tent, between Bethel and Hai,
13.4: at the place where the altar he had previously erected had been. And there Abram called on the name of the Lord.
13.5: Lot, who was traveling with Abram, also had sheep, oxen, and tents,
13.6: and the land was not large enough for them to live together, for their possessions were too great for them to remain together.
13.7: There was a quarrel between the herdsmen of Abram's flocks and the herdsmen of Lot's flocks. The Canaanites and the Perizzites were then settled in the land.
13.8: Abram said to Lot, “Please let there be no quarrel between you and me, nor between my herdsmen and your herdsmen, for we are brothers.”.
13.9: Isn't the whole country before you? Then separate yourself from me. If you go to the left, I will go to the right, and if you go to the right, I will go to the left.
13.10: Lot, lifting up his eyes, saw the whole plain of the Jordan, which was well watered: it was, before the Lord destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, like the garden of the Lord, like the land of Egypt in the direction of Zoar.
13.11: Lot chose for himself the whole plain of the Jordan, and he advanced toward the east; thus they separated from one another.
13.12: Abram lived in the land of Canaan, and Lot lived in the cities of the plain, and he pitched his tents as far as Sodom.
13.13: But the people of Sodom were very wicked and great sinners against the Lord.
13.14: The Lord said to Abram, after Lot had separated from him: Lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are toward the north and toward the south, toward the east and toward the west:
13.15: All the land that you see, I will give to you and to your descendants forever.
13.16: I will restore your posterity numerous As the dust of the earth can be counted, so can your descendants be counted.
13.17: Arise, roam the land far and wide, for I will give it to you.
13.18: Abram moved his tents and came to live by the oaks of Mamre, which are in Hebron, and there he built an altar to the Lord.

14.1: In the days of Amraphel king of Sennaar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chodorlahomor king of Elam, and Thadal king of Goim, there came
14.2: that they made war against Bara, king of Sodom, against Bersa, king of Gomorrah, against Sennaab, king of Adamah, against Shemeber, king of Zeboiim, and against the king of Bala, who is Segor.
14.3: They all gathered together in the valley of Siddim, which is the Salt Sea.
14.4: For twelve years they had been subject to Chodorlahomor, and in the thirteenth year they had rebelled.
14.5: But in the fourteenth year, Chodorlahomor set out with the kings who were with him, and they defeated the Rephaim at Astaroth-Karnaim, the Zusim at Ham, and the Emim in the plain of Caryathaim.
14.6: and the Horites in their mountain of Seir, as far as El-Pharan, which is near the desert.
14.7: Then, turning back, they came to the fountain of Judgment, which is Kadesh, and they defeated all the land of the Amalekites, as well as the Amorites who lived in Asason-Tamar.
14.8: Then the king of Sodom advanced with the king of Gomorrah, the king of Adamah, the king of Sheboiim, and the king of Balaam, who is Segor, and they drew up their battle lines against them in the valley of Siddim.,
14.9: against Chodorlahomor, king of Elam, Tadal, king of Goyim, Amraphel, king of Sennacherib, and Arioch, king of Ellasar, four kings against the five.
14.10: There were many bitumen pits in the valley of Siddim; the king of Sodom and the king of Gomorrah fled and fell into them, the rest fled into the mountains.
14.11: The winners They took all the possessions of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their provisions, and they departed.
14.12: They also took Lot, the son of Abram's brother, and his possessions, and they departed, for he was living in Sodom.
14.13: One of the fugitives came and told Abram the Hebrew, who lived by the oaks of Mamre, the Amorite, brother of Eshcol and brother of Aner, they were allies of Abram.
14.14: As soon as Abram learned that his brother had been taken captive, he assembled his most experienced men, born in his house, numbering three hundred and eighteen, and pursued the kings as far as Dan.
14.15: There, Having divided his troops to attack them at night, he and his servants defeated them and pursued them as far as Hobah, which is to the left of Damascus.
14.16: He brought back all the possessions, and he also brought back Lot, his brother, and his possessions, as well as the women and people.
14.17: As Abram returned victorious over Chodorolamor and the kings who were with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him in the valley of Shaveh, that is the King's Valley.
14.18: Melchizedek, king of Salem, brought bread and wine; he was priest of the Most High God.
14.19: He blessed Abram and said: Blessed be Abram by God Most High, who created heaven and earth.
14.20: Blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hands. And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.
14.21: The king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the people and take the possessions for yourself.".
14.22: Abram answered the king of Sodom: I have raised my hand to the Lord, the Most High God, who created heaven and earth:
14.23: From a thread to a sandal strap, I will not take anything that belongs to you so that you cannot say: I have made Abram rich.
14.24: Nothing for me. What the young men ate and the share of the men who came with me, Aner, Eschol and Mambré, they will take their share.

15.1: After these events, the word of the Lord came to Abram in a vision: Do not be afraid, Abram, I am your shield, your reward will be very great.
15.2: Abram replied, "Lord God, what will you give me? I am going away childless, and the heir to my house is Eliezer of Damascus.".
15.3: And Abram said, “Behold, you have given me no offspring, and a man attached to my house shall be my heir.”.
15.4: Then the word of the Lord came to him, saying: “This man will not be your heir, but one who will come from your own body will be your heir.”.
15.5: And, having led him outside, he said, “Lift up your eyes to the sky and count the stars, if you are able to count them.” And he said to him, “So shall your offspring be.”.
15.6: Abram believed in the Lord, and the Lord credited it to him as righteousness.
15.7: And he said to him, “I am the Lord, who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans, to give you this land to possess.”.
15.8: Abram replied, “Sovereign Lord, how will I know that I will possess it?”
15.9: And the Lord said to him, “Take a three-year-old heifer, a three-year-old female goat, a three-year-old ram, a turtledove, and a young pigeon.”.
15.10: Abram brought him all these animals and, having divided them in half, he placed each half opposite the other, but he did not divide the birds.
15.11: Birds of prey descended upon the corpses, and Abram drove them away.
15.12: As the sun set, a deep sleep fell upon Abram, and terror and profound darkness fell upon him.
15.13: The Lord said to Abram: Know well that your descendants will be strangers in a land that is not theirs, they will be enslaved and oppressed there for four hundred years.
15.14: But I will judge the nation to which they have been enslaved, and afterwards they will come out with great possessions.
15.15: You will go in peace to your fathers, you will be laid to rest in a happy old age.
15.16: In the fourth generation they will return here, for the iniquity of the Amorite is not yet complete.
15.17: When the sun had set and a deep darkness had fallen, behold, a smoking oven and a torch of fire passed between the divided animals.
15.18: On that day the Lord made a covenant with Abram, saying: I give to your descendants this land, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates:
15.19: the land of the Cinéans, the Cénézéans, the Cadmonéans,

16.1: Sarai, Abram's wife, had not borne him any children, and she had an Egyptian servant named Hagar.
16.2: Sarai said to Abram, “The Lord has prevented me from having children. Come, please, in to my servant; perhaps I can have children by her.” Abram listened to Sarai.
16.3: Sarai, Abram's wife, took SO Hagar the Egyptian, her servant, after Abram had lived ten years in the land of Canaan, and she gave her to Abram, her husband, to be his wife.
16.4: He went to Hagar, and she conceived, and when she saw that she had conceived, she looked with contempt upon her mistress.
16.5: Sarai said to Abram, “The wrong done to me is on you. I placed my servant in your arms, and when she saw that she had conceived, she looked at me with contempt. May the Lord judge between you and me.”.
16.6: Abram answered Sarai, “Behold, your servant is under your power; do to her as you please.” So Sarai mistreated her, and Hagar ran away from her home.
16.7: The angel of the Lord found her by a spring of water in the desert, by the spring that is on the road to Shur.
16.8: He said, "Hagar, servant of Sarai, where have you come from and where are you going?" She answered, "I am fleeing from Sarai, my mistress.".
16.9: The angel of the Lord said to her: Return to your mistress and humble yourself under her hand.
16.10: The angel of the Lord added: I will greatly multiply your descendants, so that they will be too numerous to count.
16.11: The angel of the Lord said to her again, “Behold, you are pregnant and will give birth to a son, and you shall name him Ishmael, for the Lord has heard your affliction.”.
16.12: This man will be a wild ass; his hand will be against everyone, and everyone's hand against him, and he will live in hostility toward all his brothers.
16.13: Hagar named the Lord who had spoken to her Atta-El-Roi because she had said: Have I seen here the God who saw me?
16.14: That is why this well was called the Lachaï-Roï well. It is located between Kadesh and Barad.
16.15: Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram named the son whom Hagar had borne Ishmael.
16.16: Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram.

17.1: When Abram reached the age of ninety-nine, the Lord appeared to him and said: I am God Almighty; walk before me and be blameless:
17.2: I will establish my covenant between me and you, and I will multiply you infinitely.
17.3: Abram fell face down on the ground, and God spoke to him thus:
17.4: This is my covenant with you: you will become the father of a multitude of nations.
17.5: You will no longer be called Abram, but your name will be Abraham, for I have made you the father of a multitude of nations.
17.6: I will make you increase exceedingly; I will make nations of you, and kings will come from you.
17.7: I establish my covenant, between me and you and your descendants after you, from generation to generation, in an everlasting covenant, to be your God and the God of your descendants after you.
17.8: I you I will give to you and your descendants after you the land where you are staying as a foreigner, all the land of Canaan, as a perpetual possession, and I will be their God.
17.9: God said to Abraham: And you, and your descendants after you, shall keep my covenant throughout all generations.
17.10: Here is the ring you must keep, the alliance between me and you, and your descendants after you: every male among you shall be circumcised.
17.11: You shall circumcise yourselves in your flesh, and this shall be the sign of the covenant between me and you.
17.12: When he is eight days old, every male among you, throughout your generations, shall be circumcised, whether born in the house or bought with money from any foreigner, who is not of your race.
17.13: The male born in the house or acquired with money must be circumcised, and my covenant will be in your flesh as an everlasting covenant.
17.14: An uncircumcised male, who has not been circumcised in his flesh, shall be cut off from his people: he shall have broken my covenant.
17.15: God said to Abraham: You shall no longer call Sarai your wife Sarai, for her name is Sarah.
17.16: I will bless her, and I will also give you a son by her; I will bless her, and she will become nations, kings of peoples will come from her.
17.17: Abraham fell facedown to the ground and laughed, saying to himself, “Can a son be born to a man a hundred years old? And can Sarah, a woman of ninety years, bear a child?”
17.18: And Abraham said to God, “Oh, let Ishmael live before you!”
17.19: God said: Yes, Sarah your wife will bear you a son, you shall name him Isaac, and I will establish my covenant with him as an everlasting covenant for his descendants after him.
17.20: As for Ishmael, I have heard you. Behold, I have blessed him, I will make him fruitful and multiply him exceedingly; he shall father twelve princes, and I will make of him a great nation.
17.21: But I will establish my covenant with Isaac, whom Sarah will bear to you next year at this time.
17.22: And having finished speaking with him, God went up from Abraham.
17.23: Abraham took Ishmael his son, and all the males born in his house and all those he had acquired with money, all the males among the people of his house, and circumcised them that very day, as God had commanded him.
17.24: Abraham was ninety-nine years old when he was circumcised,
17.25: and Ishmael, his son, was thirteen years old when he was circumcised.
17.26: On that same day, Abraham and his son Ishmael were circumcised.,
17.27: and all the men of his household, those who were born in his household and those who had been bought from foreigners, were circumcised with him.

18.1: The Lord appeared to him at the oaks of Mamre, as he sat at the entrance of the tent during the heat of the day.
18.2: He looked up and saw three men standing before him. As soon as he saw them, he ran from the entrance of the tent to meet them and, bowing down to the ground,
18.3: He said, "Lord, if I have found favor in your eyes, do not pass by your servant, I pray you.".
18.4: Please bring some water to wash your feet. Rest under this tree.,
18.5: I will take a piece of bread, and you will strengthen your hearts and continue on your way, for this is why you have passed before your servant. They replied, “Do as you have said.”.
18.6: Abraham hurried back to Sarah in the tent and said, “Quick, get three measures of flour, knead it, and make cakes.”.
18.7: Then Abraham ran to the herd and, having taken a tender and good calf, he gave it to the servant who hastened to prepare it.
18.8: He also took butter and milk, along with the calf that had been prepared, and set them before them, while he stood near them under the tree. And they ate.
18.9: Then they said to him, “Where is Sarah, your wife?” He replied: She is there, in the tent.
18.10: And he said, “I will surely return to you at this time next year, and Sarah your wife will have a son.” Sarah heard these words at the entrance of the tent, behind him.
18.11: Now Abraham and Sarah were old, advanced in years, and Sarah was beyond the age of her youth.
18.12: Sara laughed to herself, in se saying: Old as I am, will I still know pleasure? And my lord Also is old.
18.13: The Lord said to Abraham, “Why did Sarah laugh and say, ‘Will I really have a child, now that I am old?’”
18.14: Is anything too hard for the Lord? At the appointed time I will return to you, at this time of year, and Sarah will have a son.
18.15: Sara denied it, saying: I didn't laugh, because she was afraid. But he him said: No, you laughed.
18.16: These men got up to leave and turned towards Sodom; Abraham went with them to accompany them.
18.17: Then the Lord said, “Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do?”
18.18: For Abraham is to become a great and strong nation, and all the nations of the earth will be blessed in him.
18.19: I have chosen him, indeed, so that he may command his sons and his house after him to keep the way of the Lord, by practicing equity and justice, and that in this way the Lord may fulfill in favor of Abraham the promises which he has made to him.
18.20: And the Lord said: The cry that rises from Sodom and Gomorrah is very loud, and their sin very grievous.
18.21: I want to go down and see if, according to the cry that reached me, their crime has reached its peak, and if it is not so, I will know.
18.22: The men departed and went towards Sodom, and Abraham still stood before the Lord.
18.23: Abraham approached and said, “Would you also destroy the righteous with the wicked?”
18.24: Perhaps there are fifty righteous people in the city: would you destroy them also, and not spare the city for the sake of the fifty righteous people who might be in it?
18.25: Far be it from you to do such a thing, to put the righteous to death with the wicked. So it would be the same for the righteous as for the wicked. Far be it from you! Will not the Judge of all the earth render justice?
18.26: The Lord said: If I find fifty righteous people in the city of Sodom, I will forgive the whole city for their sake.
18.27: Abraham replied, "Behold, I have dared to speak to the Lord, I who am dust and ashes.".
18.28: Perhaps five of the fifty righteous men will be missing; for five men will you destroy the whole city? He said: I will not destroy it if I find forty-five.
18.29: Abraham continued speaking to him and said, "Perhaps forty righteous men will be found there." He replied, "I will not do it, for the sake of those forty.".
18.30: Abraham He said, “May the Lord not be angry if I speak. Perhaps thirty will be found.” And he said, “I will not do it if I find thirty.”.
18.31: Abraham He said, “Now I have dared to speak to the Lord. Perhaps twenty will be found.” And he said, “Because of these twenty, I will not destroy it.
18.32: Abraham He said: May the Lord not be angry, and I will speak only this once more: Perhaps ten will be found. And he said: For the sake of these ten, I will not destroy it.
18.33: The Lord left when he had finished speaking to Abraham, and Abraham returned home.

19.1: The two angels arrived in Sodom that evening, and Lot was sitting at the gate of Sodom. When Lot saw them, he got up to meet them and bowed down with his face to the ground.,
19.2: And he said, “Behold, my lords, please enter your servant’s house and spend the night there, wash your feet, and then get up early in the morning and continue on your way.” They replied, “No, we will spend the night in the square.”.
19.3: But Lot urged them so earnestly that they came to him and entered his house. He prepared a feast for them and baked unleavened bread, and they ate.
19.4: They had not yet gone to bed when the men of the city, the men of Sodom, surrounded the house, from the children to the old men, the whole people, from all parts of the city.
19.5: They called Lot and him. They said, "Where are the men who came to you last night? Bring them out to us so that we may have relations with them.".
19.6: Lot walked towards them at the entrance of the house and, having closed the door behind him,
19.7: He said: No, my brothers, I beg you, do not do evil
19.8: Look, I have two daughters who have never been with a man. Let me bring them to you, and you can do to them as you please. But do nothing to these men, for that is why they have come to take shelter under my roof.
19.9: They replied, “Get out of here!” And they added, “This man came here as a foreigner, and now he’s acting as a judge? Well, we’ll do you more harm than them!” And, driving Lot back violently, they advanced to break down the door.
19.10: THE two The men stretched out their hands and, having pulled Lot back into the house, they shut the door.
19.11: And they struck with blindness the people who were at the entrance of the house, from the least to the greatest, and they grew weary unnecessarily to look for the door.
19.12: THE two The men said to Lot, “Who else do you have here? Sons-in-law, sons and daughters, and anyone else you have in the city, bring them out of this place.”.
19.13: For we are going to destroy this place, because a great cry has arisen from its inhabitants before the Lord, and the Lord has sent us to destroy it.
19.14: Lot went out and spoke to his sons-in-law, who had taken his daughters: “Get up,” he said, “leave this place, for the Lord is about to destroy the city.” But in the eyes of his sons-in-law, he seemed to be joking.
19.15: At daybreak, the angels urged Lot, saying, “Get up, take your wife and your two daughters who are here, so that you will not perish in the punishment of the city.”.
19.16: As he delayed, these men took him by the hand, along with his wife and two daughters, for the Lord wanted to spare him, and they led him out of the city.
19.17: When they had brought them out, one of the angels said: Save yourself, for your life! Do not look back, and do not stop anywhere in the Plain; save yourself in the mountains, lest you perish!.
19.19: Behold, your servant has found favor in your sight, and you have shown me great kindness in preserving my life; but I cannot escape to the mountain, lest I be overtaken by destruction and perish.
19.20: Look, this town is close enough for me to take refuge there, and it is small, allow me to escape there, isn't it small? And let me live.
19.21: He said to him, "Here, I grant you this further favor, that you will not destroy the city of which you speak.".
19.22: Hurry and escape there, for I cannot do anything until you arrive. That is why this city was given the name Segor.
19.23: The sun rose on the earth, and Lot arrived in Segor.
19.24: Then the Lord rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah from the Lord out of heaven.
19.25: He destroyed those cities and the entire Plain, and all the inhabitants of the cities and the plants of the earth.
19.26: Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt.
19.27: Abraham got up early in the morning and went to the place where he had stood before the Lord.
19.28: He looked towards Sodom and Gomorrah, and over all the expanse of the Plain, and he saw smoke rising from the land, like the smoke of a furnace.
19.29: When God destroyed the cities of the Plain, he remembered Abraham, and he rescued Lot from the upheaval, when he overthrew the cities where Lot lived.
19.30: Lot went up from Segor and settled in the mountains, taking his two daughters with him, for he was afraid to stay in Segor, and he lived in a cave with his two daughters.
19.31: The older sister said to the younger: Our father is old, and there is no man in the land to come to us, according to the custom of all the lands.
19.32: Come, let us make our father drink wine and lie with him, so that we may preserve offspring from our father.
19.33: So they made their father drink wine that night, and the older daughter went in and lay with her father, and he did not notice when his daughter lay down or when she got up.
19.34: The next day, the older daughter said to the younger, “Last night I lay with my father. Let us make him drink wine again tonight, and you go and lie with him so that we may preserve offspring from our father.”.
19.35: That night again they made their father drink wine, and the younger daughter went and lay down beside him, and he did not notice when she lay down or when she got up.
19.36: Lot's two daughters became pregnant by their father.
19.37: The elder daughter gave birth to a son, whom she named Moab: he is the father of the Moabites, that exist to this day.
19.38: The younger daughter also had a son, whom she named Ben-Ammi: he is the father of the sons of Ammon, that exist to this day.

20.1: Abraham departed from there for the region of the South, he settled between Kadesh and Shur, and stayed for a time in Gerar.
20.2: Abraham said of Sarah, his wife: She is my sister. Abimelech, king of Gerar, sent to take Sarah.
20.3: But God came to Abimelech in a dream during the night, and said to him, “Behold, you are going to die because of the woman whom you have taken, for she is a married woman.”.
20.4: But Abimelech had not approached her, and he replied, "Lord, will you put innocent people to death?"
20.5: Didn't he tell me, "She is my sister"? And she herself told me, "He is my brother." It was with a pure heart and clean hands that I did this.
20.6: God said to him in a dream: I too know that you acted with a pure heart, so I kept you from sinning against me. That is why I did not allow you to touch her.
20.7: Now return this man's wife, for he is a prophet; he will pray for you, and you will live. If you do not return her, know that you and all your relatives will certainly die.
20.8: Abimelech got up early in the morning, called all his servants and told them all these things, and the people were filled with great fear.
20.9: Then Abimelech called Abraham and said to him, “What have you done to us? How have I wronged you, that you have brought such great sin upon me and upon my kingdom? You have done to me things that should not be done.”.
20.10: Abimelech said again to Abraham, “What were you thinking when you did this?”
20.11: Abraham replied: I thought to myself, 'Surely there is no fear of God in this land, and they will kill me because of my wife.'.
20.12: And besides, she really is my sister, she is my father's daughter, although she is not my mother's daughter, and she has become my wife.
20.13: When God caused me to wander from my father’s house, I said to Sarah, “This is the kindness you must show me: In every place we come to, say of me, ‘He is my brother.’”.
20.14: Then Abimelech took sheep and oxen, male and female servants, and gave them to Abraham, and he restored Sarah his wife to him.
20.15: Abimelech said: Behold, my land is before you; dwell wherever you please.
20.16: And he said to Sarah, “I give your brother a thousand pieces of silver; this will be a veil over your eyes for all those who are with you and for all; thus you are justified.”.
20.17: Abraham interceded with God, and God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his servants, and they had children.
20.18: For the Lord had made every womb barren in the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah, Abraham's wife.

21.1: The Lord visited Sarah, as he had said; the Lord fulfilled for Sarah what he had promised.
21.2: Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age, at the time God had appointed for him.
21.3: Abraham named the son who was born to him, whom Sarah had borne him, Isaac.
21.4: And Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old, as God had commanded him.
21.5: Abraham was one hundred years old when his son Isaac was born.
21.6: And Sarah said: God has given me something to laugh about; whoever hears about it will laugh at me.
21.7: She added: Who would have said to Abraham, “Sarah will nurse children”? For I have borne him a son in his old age.
21.8: The child grew up, and he was weaned. Abraham held a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned.
21.9: Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, laughing,
21.10: And she said to Abraham, “Cast out this slave woman and her son, for the son of this slave woman shall not inherit with my son, with Isaac.”.
21.11: This statement greatly displeased Abraham because of his son Ishmael .
21.12: But God said to Abraham, “Do not be displeased on account of the child and your servant. Whatever Sarah asks of you, consent to it, for it is from Isaac that offspring will be born who will bear your name.”.
21.13: Nevertheless, I will also make a nation of the son of the servant girl, because he was born of you.
21.14: Abraham got up early in the morning, took some bread and a skin of water, gave them to Hagar, and put them on her shoulder, he handed it to him He also sent the child away, and she left, wandering in the desert of Beersheba.
21.15: When the water in the skin was gone, she threw the child under one of the bushes,
21.16: And she went and sat opposite him, a bowshot away, for she said, “I do not want to see the child die.” So she sat opposite him, raised her voice, and wept.
21.17: God heard the voice of the child, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven, saying, “What is the matter with you, Hagar? Do not be afraid, for God has heard the voice of the child where he is.”.
21.18: Get up, lift up the child, take him by the hand, for I will make him into a great nation.
21.19: And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; she went and filled the skin with water and gave the child a drink.
21.20: God was with the child, and he grew up and lived in the desert and became an archer.
21.21: He lived in the desert of Pharan, and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt.
21.22: At that time, Abimelech, accompanied by Philol, the commander of his army, spoke to Abraham, saying: God is with you in all that you do.
21.23: Swear to me here, by the name of God, that you will not deceive me, nor my children, nor my grandchildren, but that you will have for me and for this country where you are staying the same kindness that I have shown to you.
21.24: Abraham said: I will swear it.
21.25: But Abraham reproached Abimelech concerning a well of water which Abimelech's servants had seized by force.
21.26: Abimelech replied: I do not know who did this; you yourself did not inform me of it, and I only heard about it today.
21.27: And Abraham took sheep and oxen, and gave them to Abimelech, and the two of them made a covenant.
21.28: Abraham set apart seven young ewes from the flock,
21.29: And Abimelech said to Abraham, "What are these seven young ewes that you have set apart?"
21.30: He replied: You will accept these seven young ewes from my hand, so that it may be a testimony for me that I dug this well.
21.31: That is why this place was called Beersheba, because it was there that they both took their oath.
21.32: That's how they They made an alliance with Beersheba. After that, Abimelech arose, with Philol, the commander of his army, and they returned to the land of the Philistines.
21.33: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba, and there he called on the name of the Lord, the eternal God,
21.34: And Abraham stayed a long time in the land of the Philistines.

22.1: After this, God tested Abraham and said to him: Abraham. He answered: Here I am.
22.2: And God said, “Take your son, your only son, whom you love—Isaac—and go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains that I will show you.”.
22.3: Abraham got up early in the morning and, having saddled his donkey, he took with him two of his servants and his son Isaac, he split the wood for the burnt offering and set out to go to the place that God had told him about.
22.4: On the third day, Abraham raised his eyes and saw the place from afar,
22.5: And Abraham said to his servants, “Stay here with the donkey; I and the child want to go over there and worship, and then we will come back to you.”.
22.6: And Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering and placed it on his son Isaac, while he himself carried the fire and the knife in his hand, and the two of them went away together.
22.7: Isaac spoke to Abraham, his father, and said, “My father.” He answered, “Here I am, my son.” And Isaac said, “Here are the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?”
22.8: Abraham replied, "God will see to it that a lamb for the burnt offering is found, my son." And the two of them went on together.
22.9: When they arrived at the place God had designated for him, Abraham erected the altar there and arranged the wood, then he bound Isaac, his son, and laid him on the altar, on top of the wood.
22.10: And Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son.
22.11: Then the angel of the Lord called to him from heaven and said, “Abraham! Abraham!” He answered, “Here I am.”.
22.12: And the angel said, “Do not lay a hand on the child or do anything to him, for now I know that you fear God, because you have not withheld from me your son, your only son.”.
22.13: Abraham, having lifted his eyes, saw behind him a ram caught in a bush by its horns, and Abraham went and took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering in place of his son.
22.14: And Abraham named that place Lord Yireh, from which it is said to this day: On the mountain of the Lord he will be seen.
22.15: The angel of the Lord called to Abraham from heaven a second time, saying:
22.16: I have sworn by myself, says the Lord: because you have done this, and have not withheld from me your son, your only son,
22.17: I will bless you, and I will multiply your descendants as the stars of heaven and as the sand that is on the seashore, and your descendants will possess the gate of their enemies.
22.18: Through your offspring all nations of the earth will be blessed, because you have obeyed my voice.
22.19: Abraham returned to his servants, and they got up and went together to Beersheba. And Abraham lived in Beersheba.
22.20: After these events, Abraham received this news: Behold, Melchah has also borne sons to Nahor, your brother:
22.21: Hus, his firstborn, Buz, his brother, Camuel, father of Aram,
22.22: Cased, Azau, Pheldas, Jedlaph and Bathuel.
22.23: Bathuel was the father of Rebekah. These were the eight sons that Melcha bore to Nahor, Abraham's brother.
22.24: His concubine, named Roma, also had children: Tabée, Gaham, Taas and Maacha.

23.1: Sara lived one hundred and twenty-seven years: these are the years of her life.
23.2: Sarah died at Kiriath-arbe, which is Hebron, in the land of Canaan, and Abraham came to mourn for Sarah and to weep for her.
23.3: Then Abraham rose from before his dead man and spoke thus to the sons of Heth:
23.4: I am a stranger and a guest among you; grant me a tomb among you, so that I may bury my deceased wife there.
23.5: The sons of Heth answered Abraham, saying:
23.6: Listen to us, my lord, you are a prince of God in our midst, bury your dead in the most beautiful of our tombs, none of us will refuse you his tomb to lay your dead there.
23.7: Then Abraham arose and bowed down before the people of the land, before the sons of Heth,
23.8: He spoke to them in these words: If you want me to remove my dead body from before me to bury him, listen to me and pray for me, Ephron son of Seor,
23.9: to give me Macpela's cave, which belongs to him and is at the end of his field, to give it to me in your presence for the money it is worth, as a burial place that is mine.
23.10: Now Ephron was sitting among the Hittites. Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the presence of the Hittites, of all who entered the gate of his city, he said to him:
23.11: No, my lord, listen to me: I give you the field and I give you the cave that is in it, I give it to you in the sight of the sons of my people, bury your dead.
23.12: Abraham bowed down before the people of the land,
23.13: And he spoke thus to Ephron in the presence of the people of the land: Let it please you only to listen to me: I give the price of the field, receive it from me, and I will bury my dead there.
23.15: My lord, listen to me: what is a piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver between you and me? Bury your dead.
23.16: Abraham listened to Ephron, and Abraham weighed out to Ephron the money he had mentioned in the presence of the sons of Heth, know four hundred shekels of silver, current currency among the merchant.
23.17: Thus the field of Ephron, which is at Machpelah, opposite Mamre, the field and the cave that is in it, as well as all the trees that are in the field and in its surrounding borders,
23.18: became the property of Abraham, in the eyes of the sons of Heth, of all who entered by the gate of the city.
23.19: After this, Abraham buried Sarah, his wife, in the cave of Machpelah, opposite Mamre, which is Hebron, in the land of Canaan.
23.20: The field, with the cave in it, remained Abraham's property as a burial place, coming from the sons of Heth.

24.1: Abraham was old, advanced in years, and the Lord had blessed Abraham in all things.
24.2: And Abraham said to his servant, the oldest of his household, who was in charge of all his possessions, “Put your hand under my thigh,
24.3: and I will make you swear by the Lord, the God of heaven and the God of earth, that you will not take a wife for my son from among the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell,
24.4: but it will be in my country and in my homeland that you will go and take a wife for my son, for Isaac.
24.5: The servant replied, "Perhaps the woman will not want to follow me to this country; shall I bring your son back to the country from which you came?"
24.6: Abraham said to him, “Beware of bringing my son back there.”.
24.7: The Lord, the God of heaven, who took me from my father’s house and from the land of my birth, who spoke to me and swore to me, saying, “I will give this land to your offspring,” he himself will send his angel before you, and you shall take a wife for my son from there.
24.8: If the woman does not want to follow you, you will be released from this oath that I am asking of you, but you will not bring my son back there.
24.9: Then the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham, his master, and swore an oath to him concerning this matter.
24.10: The servant took ten of his master's camels and set out, having at his disposal all his master's possessions. He got up and went to Mesopotamia, to the city of Nahor.
24.11: He made the camels kneel outside the city, near a well, towards evening, at the time when women go out to draw water.
24.12: And he said, “Lord, God of Abraham, my master, please let me meet today.” what I desire , and show kindness to my master Abraham.
24.13: Here I am standing by the spring of water, and the daughters of the townspeople are going out to draw water.
24.14: Let the young woman to whom I say, “Please lower your pitcher so I may drink,” and who answers, “Drink, and I will also give your camels a drink,” be the one you have chosen for your servant Isaac. And by this I will know that you have shown kindness to my master.
24.15: He had not yet finished speaking, and behold, Rebecca, daughter of Bathuel, son of Melchah, wife of Nahor, Abraham's brother, came out with her pitcher on her shoulder.
24.16: The young girl was very beautiful, she was a virgin, and no man had known her. She went down to the spring, filled her jug, and came back up.
24.17: The servant ran to meet her and said, "Let me drink a little water from your jug.".
24.18: She replied: Drink, my lord, and, hastening to lower her pitcher onto her hand, she gave him a drink.
24.19: When she had finished giving him a drink, she said, “I will draw water too.” water for the camels, until they have drunk enough.
24.20: And she quickly emptied her jug into the watering trough, and ran again to the well to draw water, and she drew water for all the camels.
24.21: The man looked at her in silence, to know whether the Lord had made his journey successful, or not.
24.22: When the camels had finished drinking, the man took a gold ring weighing half a shekel, and two bracelets weighing ten shekels of gold,
24.23: And he said, "Whose daughter are you? Tell me. Is there a place in your father's house where we can spend the night?"
24.24: She replied: I am the daughter of Bathuel, the son of Melchah, whom she bore to Nahor.
24.25: She added: We have plenty of straw and fodder, and also room to spend the night.
24.26: Then this man bowed down and prostrated himself before the Lord,
24.27: And he said, “Blessed be the Lord, the God of Abraham my master, who has not failed in his kindness and faithfulness to my master. As for me, the Lord led me by the way to my master’s brothers.”.
24.28: The young girl ran to tell her mother what had happened.
24.29: Rebecca had a brother named Laban. Laban ran outside to this man, near the spring.
24.30: He had seen the ring and the bracelets on his sister's hands, and he had heard his sister Rebecca say, "The man told me this." So he went to the man, who was standing by the camels at the spring,
24.31: And he said, “Come, you who are blessed by the Lord, why are you standing outside? I have prepared the house and a place for the camels.”.
24.32: And the man went into the house. Laban unloaded the camels, and he gave straw and fodder to the camels, and water to wash the feet of the man and the feet of the people who were with him.
24.33: Then he served him food, but the man said, "I will not eat until I have said what I have to say." "Speak," said Laban.
24.35: The Lord has blessed my master abundantly, and he has become powerful. He has given him sheep and oxen, male and female servants, camels and donkeys.
24.36: Sarah, my master's wife, bore my master a son in her old age, and he gave him all his possessions.
24.37: My master made me swear an oath, saying: You shall not take for my son a wife from among the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell.
24.38: But you will go to my father's house and to my relatives, and you will take there a woman for my son.
24.39: I said to my master: Perhaps the woman will not want to follow me.
24.40: And he answered me: The Lord, before whom I walk, will send his angel with you and will make your journey successful, and you shall take for my son a wife from my relatives and from my father's house.
24.41: You will be released from the oath you are making to me, once you have gone to my family; if it is not granted to you, you will be released from the oath I am asking of you.
24.42: Upon arriving at the spring today, I said: Lord, God of my master Abraham, if you deign to grant success to the journey I am making,
24.43: Here I am, standing by the spring of water, when the young girl comes out to draw water and I say to her, 'Please let me drink a little water from your jug,',
24.44: and who will answer me: Drink yourself, and I will also draw water for your camels, let her be the woman whom the Lord has destined for my master's son.
24.45: I had not yet finished speaking in my heart, behold, Rebecca came out, her jug on her shoulder, she went down to the spring and drew water, and I said to her: Give me a drink, I beg you.
24.46: She immediately lowered her jug from her shoulder, me He said, "Drink, and I will also give your camels water." I have SO She drank, and she also gave water to the camels.
24.47: And I questioned her, saying, "Whose daughter are you?" She answered, "I am the daughter of Bathuel, the son of Nahor, whom Melcha bore to him." So I put the ring in her nose and the bracelets on her hands.
24.48: Then I bowed down and prostrated myself before the Lord, and I blessed the Lord, the God of my master Abraham, who led me in the right way to take the daughter of my master's brother for his son.
24.49: Now, if you want to show kindness and loyalty to my master, tell me so; if not, tell me so. Again , and I will turn right or left.
24.50: Laban and Bathuel replied, saying, “This is from the Lord; we can neither say good nor bad to you.”.
24.51: Here is Rebecca in front of you, take- there and go, let her be the wife of your master's son, as the Lord has said.
24.52: When Abraham's servant heard these words, he bowed down to the ground before the Lord.
24.53: And the servant brought out articles of silver, articles of gold and clothing, which he gave to Rebecca, and he also gave rich gifts to her brother and her mother.
