24.54: Then they ate and drank, he and the people who were with him, and they passed away there at night. In the morning, when they got up, the servant said, “Let me go back to my master.”.
24.55: Rebecca's brother and mother said: Let the young girl stay with us for a few more days, about ten, after which she will leave.
24.56: He replied to them, “Do not delay me, since the Lord has made my journey successful; let me go, so that I may return to my master.”.
24.57: They said, "Let's call the girl and ask her what she wants.".
24.58: So they called Rebecca and said to her, "Do you want to go with this man?" She replied, "I will go.".
24.59: So they dismissed Rebecca, their sister, and her nurse, along with Abraham's servant and his men.
24.60: They blessed Rebecca and said to her: O our sister, may you become thousands upon thousands. May your descendants possess the gate of her enemies.
24.61: Then Rebecca and her maids got up, mounted the camels, and followed the man. And the servant took Rebecca with him and set off.
24.62: However, Isaac had returned from the well of Chai-Roi, and he was living in the land of the South.
24.63: One evening when Isaac had gone out into the fields to meditate, he looked up and saw camels coming.
24.64: Rebecca also looked up and, having caught sight of Isaac, she jumped down from her camel.
24.65: She said to the servant, "Who is that man coming toward us in the fields?" The servant replied, "He is my master." And she took her veil and covered herself.
24.66: The servant told Isaac all the things he had done.
24.67: And Isaac brought Rebekah into the tent of Sarah, his mother. He took Rebekah, who became his wife, and he loved her, and Isaac was comforted after the death of his mother.

25.1: Abraham took another wife, named Cetura.
25.2: And she bore him Zamran, Jecsan, Madan, Midian, Jesboc, and Sué.
25.3: Jecsan fathered Saba and Dadan; the sons of Dadan were the Assurim, the Latusim, and the Laomim.
25.4: The sons of Midian were Ephah, Opher, Enoch, Abida, and Eldaah. These were all the sons of Keturah.
25.5: Abraham gave all his possessions to Isaac:
25.6: As for the sons of his concubines, he gave them gifts, and he sent them away during his lifetime from his son Isaac, to the east, to the land of the East.
25.7: These are the days of the years of Abraham's life: he lived one hundred and seventy-five years.
25.8: Abraham breathed his last and died at a good old age, aged and satisfied. days , and he was reunited with his people.
25.9: Isaac and Ishmael, his sons, buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron, son of Seor the Hittite, who is vis-à-vis Mamre:
25.10: This was the field that Abraham had bought from the sons of Heth. There Abraham was buried, with Sarah, his wife.
25.11: After Abraham's death, God blessed his son Isaac, and Isaac lived near the well of Beer-lahai-roi.
25.12: This is the story of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's servant, bore to Abraham.
25.13: These are the names of the sons of Ishmael, according to the names of their descendants: Ishmael's firstborn, Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mabsam,
25.15: Hadad, Thema, Jethur, Naphis and Cedma.
25.16: These are the sons of Ishmael, these are their names, according to their villages and their encampments: these were the twelve chiefs of their tribes.
25.17: These are the years of Ishmael's life: one hundred and thirty-seven years, then he breathed his last and died and was reunited with his people.
25.18: His sons lived from Hevila to Shur, which is opposite Egypt, in the direction of Assyria. He spread out opposite all his brothers.
25.19: This is the story of Isaac, Abraham's son. Abraham begot Isaac.
25.20: Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah, daughter of Bathuel the Aramean of Paddan-Aram, and sister of Laban the Aramean, as his wife.
25.21: Isaac pleaded with the Lord on behalf of his wife, because she was barren; the Lord answered him, and Rebecca, his wife, conceived.
25.22: The children struggled together within her, and she said, “If this is so, why am I…” pregnant She went to consult the Lord,
25.23: And the Lord said to him, “Two nations are in your womb, two peoples from within you shall be separated; one people shall prevail over the other, and the greater shall serve the lesser.”.
25.24: The time came for her to give birth, and behold, there were two twins in her womb.
25.25: The one who came out first was red, all like a hairy garment, and they called him Esau. Afterward his brother came out, holding Esau's heel in his hand, and he was named Jacob.
25.26: Isaac was sixty years old when they were born.
25.27: These children grew up. Esau became a skilled hunter, a man of the fields, but Jacob was a peaceful man, dwelling in the tent.
25.28: Isaac took a liking to Esau because he loved game, and Rebecca's affection was for Jacob.
25.29: As Jacob was making soup, Esau arrived from the fields, exhausted.
25.30: Esau said to Jacob, “Let me eat some of that red stew, that red stew, for I am tired.” That is why Esau was called Edom.
25.31: Jacob said: First sell me your birthright.
25.32: Esau replied, "Behold, I am about to die; what good will my birthright do me?"
25.33: And Jacob said, "Swear to me first." So he swore and sold his birthright to Jacob.
25.34: Then Jacob gave Esau bread and lentil stew, and he ate and drank, then got up and went away. Thus Esau despised his birthright.

26.1: There was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that had taken place in the time of Abraham, and Isaac went to Gerar, to Abimelech, king of the Philistines.
26.2: The Lord appeared to him and said, “Do not go down to Egypt, but stay in the land I will tell you about.”.
26.3: Stay in this land, I will be with you and I will bless you, for I will give all these lands to you and to your offspring, and I will fulfill the oath that I swore to Abraham your father.
26.4: I will multiply your descendants like the stars of heaven, and I will give to your descendants all these lands, and through your descendants all nations of the earth will be blessed.,
26.5: because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my order, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.
26.7: The locals asked him about his wife, and he would say, "She is my sister," because he was afraid to say, "My wife," for fear of..., he thought, that the local people do not kill me because of Rebecca, for she was beautiful.
26.8: Like his stay to Gerare As it continued, it happened that Abimelech, king of the Philistines, looking out of the window, saw Isaac caressing Rebecca, his wife.
26.9: Abimelech called Isaac and said, "She can only be your wife. How could you say, 'She is my sister'?" Isaac answered him, "Because I thought to myself, 'I am afraid I may die because of her.'".
26.10: And Abimelech said, “What have you done to us? For a man from the people could very well have slept with your wife, and you would have brought sin upon us.”.
26.11: Then Abimelech gave this order to all the people: Whoever touches this man or his wife shall be put to death.
26.12: Isaac sowed seed in that land, and in that year he reaped a hundredfold. The Lord blessed him,
26.13: And this man became rich, and he went on getting richer and richer, until he became very rich.
26.14: He had flocks of small cattle and flocks of large cattle and many servants, and the Philistines envied him.
26.15: All the wells that his father’s servants had dug in the time of his father Abraham, the Philistines stopped up, filling them with earth.
26.16: And Abimelech said to Isaac, “Go away from us, for you have become much more powerful than we are.”.
26.17: Isaac went away and, having established his camp in the valley of Gerares, he remained there.
26.18: Isaac reopened the wells that had been dug in the days of Abraham his father, and that the Philistines had stopped up after Abraham’s death, and he gave them the same names that his father had given them.
26.19: Isaac's servants dug Again in the valley and there they found a well of fresh water.
26.20: And the shepherds of Geraras quarreled with the shepherds of Isaac, saying, "The water is ours." And he named the well Eseq, because they had had a dispute with him.
26.21: His servants dug another well, about which there was yet another quarrel, and he named it Sitna.
26.22: Leaving there, he dug another well, over which there was no more quarreling, and he named it Rehoboth: For now, he said, the Lord has set us free, and we shall prosper in the land.
26.23: From there, he went back up to Beersheba.
26.24: The Lord appeared to him that night and said, “I am the God of Abraham your father; do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bless you and multiply your descendants for the sake of Abraham my servant.”.
26.25: He erected an altar there and called on the name of the Lord, then pitched his tent there, and Isaac's servants dug a well there.
26.26: Abimelech came to him from Gerares, with Ahazath, his friend, and Philol, commander of his army.
26.27: Isaac said to them, "Why have you come to me, you who hate me and who drove me away from your home?"
26.28: They replied: We have clearly seen that the Lord is with you, and we have said: Let there be an oath between us, between us and you, and let us make a covenant with you.
26.29: Swear that you will not harm us, just as we have not harmed you, and have only done you good, and let you go in peace. You are now blessed by the Lord.
26.30: Isaac prepared a feast for them, and they ate and drank.
26.31: And, having risen early in the morning, they swore an oath to one another, then Isaac dismissed them and they departed from his house in peace.
26.32: That same day, Isaac's servants came to him with news of the well they were digging, saying: We have found water.
26.33: And he named the well Schibéa. That is why the city is called Beersabée to this day.
26.34: Esau, aged forty years, took as wives Judith, daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath, daughter of Elon the Hittite.
26.35: They were a source of bitterness for Isaac and Rebecca.

27.1: Isaac had grown old, and his eyes had grown dim so that he could no longer see. He called Esau, his older son, and said to him, “My son.” Esau answered him, “Here I am.”.
27.2: Isaac said: Behold, I am old, and I do not know the day of my death.
27.3: Now then, take your weapons, your quiver and your bow, go out into the countryside and kill some game for me.
27.4: Make me a good dish, according to my taste, and bring it to me, so that I may eat it, and that my soul may bless you before I die.
27.5: Rebecca overheard Isaac speaking to his son Esau. And Esau went out into the countryside to hunt game and bring it back.
27.6: Then Rebecca spoke to her son Jacob, saying: “Look, I heard your father speaking to your brother Esau:
27.7: Bring me some game and make me a good dish, so that I may eat it and bless you before the Lord before I die.
27.8: Now, my son, listen to my voice in what I am going to command you.
27.9: Go to the herd and bring me two fine kids; I'll make a good dish for your father, according to his taste.,
27.10: and you shall take it to your father, and he shall eat of it, so that he may bless you before he dies.
27.11: Jacob answered his mother Rebecca, “Look, my brother Esau is hairy, but I have smooth skin.”.
27.12: Perhaps my father will touch me, and I will appear in his eyes as having mocked him, and I will bring a curse upon myself instead of a blessing.
27.13: His mother said to him: I take your curse upon myself, my son. Just listen to my voice and go and get me the kids.
27.14: Jacob went and got them and brought them to his mother, who made a good dish out of them, according to his father's taste.
27.15: And Rebecca took the finest clothes of Esau, her older son, which she had in the house, and she dressed Jacob, her younger son, in them.
27.16: Then she covered his hands with the skin of the kids, as well as the smooth part of his neck.
27.17: And she put into the hand of Jacob, her son, the good food and the bread that she had prepared.
27.18: He came to his father and said, “My father.” “Here I am,” said Isaac, “who are you, my son?”
27.19: Jacob answered his father, “I am Esau, your firstborn; I have done as you told me. Now rise, I pray you, sit down and eat of my game, so that your soul may bless me.”.
27.20: Isaac said to his son, "How did you find him so quickly, my son?" Jacob replied, "Because the Lord your God brought him before me.".
27.21: And Isaac said to Jacob, “Come here, my son, so that I may touch you and know whether you are really my son Esau or not.”.
27.22: Jacob approached Isaac, his father, who touched him and said, "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau.".
27.23: He did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy, like the hands of Esau, his brother, and he blessed him.
27.24: He said, "Are you really my son Esau?" "I am," replied Jacob.
27.25: And Isaac said, “Serve me, that I may eat some of my son’s game and that my soul may bless you.” So Jacob served him, and he ate; he also brought him wine, and he drank.
27.26: Then Isaac, his father, said to him, “Come here and kiss me, my son.”.
27.27: Jacob approached and kissed him, and Isaac smelled the scent of his garments, and he blessed him, saying: Behold, the smell of my son is like the smell of a field which the Lord has blessed.
27.28: May God give you dew from heaven and fatness from the earth, and an abundance of wheat and wine.
27.29: Let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you. Be lord over your brothers, and let your mother's sons bow down to you. Cursed be he who curses you, and blessed be he who blesses you.
27.30: Isaac had finished blessing Jacob, and Jacob had just left Isaac, his father, when Esau, his brother, returned from hunting.
27.31: He too prepared a good dish, and brought it to his father, and said to his father, “Let my father get up and eat some of his son’s game, so that your soul may bless me.”.
27.32: Isaac, his father, said to him, “Who are you?” He answered, “I am your son, your firstborn, Esau.”.
27.33: Isaac was seized with extreme terror, and he said: Who then is this that hunted game and brought it to me? I ate it all before you came, and I blessed it, and indeed it will be blessed.
27.34: When Esau heard his father's words, he cried out with a loud and bitter lament, and said to his father, "Bless me, my father.".
27.35: Isaac said: Your brother came deceitfully and took your blessing.
27.36: Esau said, “Is it because his name is Jacob that he has supplanted me twice? He took my birthright, and now he has taken my blessing.” He added, “Have you not reserved a blessing for me?”
27.37: Isaac answered and said to Esau, “Behold, I have made him your master and have given him all his brothers as servants, and have provided him with wheat and wine, and what can I do for you, my son?”
27.38: Esau said to his father, “Is this the only blessing you have, my father? Bless me too, my father.” And Esau lifted up his voice and wept.
27.39: Isaac, his father, answered him: Your dwelling will be devoid of the fatness of the earth, devoid of the dew that comes down from heaven.
27.40: You will live by your sword, and you will be enslaved to your brother, but it will come to pass that, by shaking off his yoke, you will break him from your neck.
27.41: Esau conceived hatred against Jacob because of the blessing that his father had given him, and Esau said in his heart, "The days of mourning for my father are approaching; then I will kill Jacob my brother.".
27.42: Rebecca was told what her older son Esau had said. She summoned Jacob, her younger son, and said to him, “Your brother Esau wants to take revenge on you by killing you.”.
27.43: Now then, my son, listen to my voice: get up, flee to Laban, my brother, in Haran,
27.44: and you will stay with him for a while, until your brother's anger has subsided,
27.45: until your brother's anger has turned away from you, and he has forgotten what you did to him, then I will send for you from there. Why should I be deprived of both of you in one day?
27.46: Rebecca said to Isaac, “I am disgusted with my life because of the daughters of Heth. If Jacob takes a wife like these, from among the daughters of Heth, from among the daughters of this land, what good will my life be to me?”

28.1: Isaac called Jacob and blessed him, and gave him this order: You shall not take as your wife one of the daughters of Canaan.
28.2: Get up, go to Paddan-Aram, to Bethuel, your mother’s father, and take a wife from among the daughters of Laban, your mother’s brother.
28.3: May God Almighty bless you, and make you increase and multiply, so that you may become a multitude of peoples.
28.4: May he give you the blessing of Abraham, to you and to your descendants with you, so that you may possess the land where you are staying, which God gave to Abraham.
28.5: And Isaac dismissed Jacob, who went to Paddan-Aram, to Laban, son of Bethuel the Aramean, brother of Rebekah, the mother of Jacob and Esau.
28.6: Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him to Paddan-Aram to take a wife from there, and that when he blessed him, he had given him this order: You shall not take one of the daughters of Canaan as your wife,
28.7: and that Jacob, obeying his father and mother, had gone to Paddan-Aram.
28.8: Esau saw that the daughters of Canaan displeased Isaac, his father,
28.9: And Esau went to Ishmael, and he took as his wife, besides the wives he already had, Mahelath, daughter of Ishmael, son of Abraham, and sister of Nabaioth.
28.10: Jacob left Beersheba and went to Haran.
28.11: He arrived at a place and spent the night there, because the sun had set. Taking one of the stones that was there, he made it his pillow, and he lay down in that place.
28.12: He had a dream: and behold, a ladder was set upon the earth, and its top reached to heaven, and behold, angels of God were ascending and descending upon it,
28.13: And the Lord stood on high. He said, “I am the Lord, the God of Abraham your father and the God of Isaac. This land on which you lie I will give to you, to you and to your posterity.
28.14: Your descendants will be like the dust of the earth, and you will spread out to the west and to the east, to the north and to the south, and all the families of the earth will be blessed in you and in your offspring.
28.15: Behold, I am with you, and I will keep you wherever you go, and I will bring you back to this land. For I will not leave you until I have done what I have promised you.
28.16: Jacob awoke from his sleep and said, “Surely the Lord is in this place, and I did not know it.”.
28.17: Seized with fear, he added: How awesome is this place! This is truly the house of God, this is the gate of heaven.
28.18: Having risen early in the morning, Jacob took the stone which he had used as his pillow, set it up as a monument and poured oil on top of it.
28.19: He named this place Bethel, but originally the town was called Luz.
28.20: And Jacob made a vow, saying: If God will be with me and will keep me on this journey I am making, and will give me bread to eat and clothes to wear,
28.21: And if I return happily to my father's house, the Lord will be my God.,
28.22: This stone that I have set up as a monument will be a house of God, and I will pay you a tithe of all that you give me.

29.1: Jacob resumed his journey and went to the land of the people of the East.
29.2: He looked, and behold, there was a well in the countryside, and behold, there were three flocks of sheep lying beside it, for it was at this well that the flocks were watered, and the stone that was over the opening of the well was large.
29.3: All the flocks would gather there, they would roll the stone off the well opening, they would water the flocks, and then they would put the stone back in its place over the well opening.
29.4: Jacob said to the shepherds, "My brothers, where are you from?" They replied, "We are from Haran.".
29.5: He said to them, “Do you know Laban, son of Nahor?” They replied, “We know him.”.
29.6: He said to them, “Is he well?” They answered, “He is well, and here is Rachel, his daughter, coming with the sheep.”.
29.7: He said: Behold, it is still broad daylight, and it is not time to gather the flocks; water the sheep and return to pasture them.
29.8: They replied: We cannot do it until all the flocks are gathered together and the stone is rolled away from the well opening, then we will water the sheep.
29.9: He was still talking with them when Rachel arrived with her father's sheep, for she was a shepherdess.
29.10: As soon as Jacob saw Rachel, the daughter of Laban, his mother's brother, and the sheep of Laban, his mother's brother, he approached, rolled the stone from the mouth of the well, and watered the sheep of Laban, his mother's brother.
29.11: And Jacob kissed Rachel, and he lifted up his voice and wept.
29.12: Jacob told Rachel that he was her father's brother, that he was Rebecca's son, and she ran to tell her father.
29.13: When Laban heard about Jacob, his sister's son, he ran to meet him, embraced him, kissed him, and brought him into his house. Jacob told Laban all these things,
29.14: And Laban said to him, “Yes, you are my own flesh and blood.” And Jacob stayed with him for a whole month.
29.15: Then Laban said to Jacob, “Just because you are my brother, will you serve me for free? Tell me what your wages will be.”.
29.16: Now Laban had two daughters, the elder was named Leah, and the younger Rachel.
29.17: Léa had sick eyes, but Rachel was beautiful in stature and face.
29.18: Because Jacob loved Rachel, he said, “I will serve you seven years for Rachel, your younger daughter.”.
29.19: And Laban said: It is better to give it to you than to give it to another; stay with me.
29.20: And Jacob served for Rachel seven years, and they seemed to him but a few days, because he loved her.
29.21: Jacob said to Laban, “Give me my wife, for my time is fulfilled, and I will go to her.”.
29.22: Laban gathered all the people of the place and held a feast,
29.23: And in the evening, taking Leah, his daughter, he brought her to Jacob, who went to her.
29.24: And Laban gave his servant Zelphah as a servant to Leah, his daughter.
29.25: When morning came, there was Leah. And Jacob said to Laban, “What have you done to me? Wasn’t it for Rachel that I served you? Why have you deceived me?”
29.26: Laban replied: It is not the custom in our country to give the younger daughter before the older one.
29.27: Complete this week, and we will also give you the other for the service you will do with me for another seven years.
29.28: Jacob did so, and he completed Leah's week, and then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel as a wife.
29.29: And Laban gave his servant Balaam to Rachel, his daughter, as a servant.
29.30: Jacob also went in to Rachel and loved her more than Leah; he served Laban for another seven years.
29.31: The Lord saw that Leah was hated, and he made her fruitful, but Rachel was barren.
29.32: Leah conceived and gave birth to a son, and she named him Reuben, for she said, “The Lord has seen my affliction; now my husband will love me.”.
29.33: She conceived again and gave birth to a son, and she said, “The Lord has heard that I am hated, and he has given me this one too.” And she named him Simeon.
29.34: She conceived again and gave birth to a son, and she said, "This time my husband will be devoted to me, for I have borne him three sons." Therefore he was named Levi.
29.35: She conceived again and gave birth to a son, and she said, “This time I will praise the Lord.” Therefore she named him Judah. And she stopped having children.

30.1: When Rachel saw that she was not bearing children to Jacob, she was jealous of her sister, and she said to Jacob, “Give me children, or I shall die.”.
30.2: Jacob's anger burned against Rachel, and he said, "Am I in the place of God, who has withheld fertility from you?"
30.3: She said: Here is my servant Bala, go to her, let her bear children on my knees, and through her I too shall have a family.
30.4: And she gave him Balaam, her servant, as a wife, and Jacob went in to her.
30.5: Bala conceived and bore a son to Jacob.
30.6: And Rachel said, “God has vindicated me; he has even heard my voice and given me a son.” Therefore she named him Dan.
30.7: Bahla, Rachel's servant, conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.
30.8: And Rachel said, “I have wrestled with God against my sister, and I have prevailed.” And she named him Naphtali.
30.9: When Leah saw that she had stopped having children, she took Zelpah, her servant, and gave her to Jacob as a wife.
30.10: Zelpha, Leah's servant, bore Jacob a son,
30.11: And Léa said: How lucky, and she named him Gad.
30.12: Zelpha, Leah's servant, bore Jacob a second son,
30.13: And Leah said: For my happiness, for the girls will call me blessed. And she named him Aser.
30.14: Reuben went out at the time of the wheat harvest and, having found mandrakes in the fields, he brought them to Leah, his mother. Then Rachel said to Leah, “Please give me some of your son’s mandrakes.”.
30.15: She replied, "Is it a small thing that you have taken my husband, that you should also take my son's mandrakes?" And Rachel said, "Well then, let him lie with you tonight for your son's mandrakes.".
30.16: That evening, as Jacob was returning from the fields, Leah went out to meet him and him He said, "You will come to me, for I have hired you for my son's mandrakes." And he slept with her that night.
30.17: God answered Leah's prayer, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.,
30.18: And Leah said, “God has given me my reward because I gave my servant to my husband.” And she named him Issachar.
30.19: Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son,
30.20: And she said, “God has given me a good gift; this time my husband will dwell with me, since I have borne him six sons.” And she named him Zebulun.
30.21: She then gave birth to a daughter, whom she named Dina.
30.22: God remembered Rachel, he answered her prayer and made her fruitful.
30.23: She conceived and gave birth to a son, and she said, “God has taken away my disgrace.”.
30.24: And she named him Joseph, saying, “May the Lord add to me…” Again another son.
30.25: When Rachel had given birth to Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, “Let me go, so that I may return home to my own country.”.
30.26: Give me my wives and my children, for whom I have served you, and I will go away, for you know what service I have done for you.
30.27: Laban said to him, “If I have found favor in your eyes… I have seen that the Lord has blessed me because of you,
30.28: Tell me your salary, and I will give it to you.
30.29: Jacob said to him, "You yourself know how I served you and what became of your livestock with me.".
30.30: For your possessions were small before I came, but they have increased exceedingly, and the Lord has blessed you wherever I have been. Now when shall I also work for my own household?
30.31: Laban He said, “What shall I give you?” And Jacob said, “You shall give me nothing. If you grant me what I say, I will again tend your flock and keep it.”.
30.32: I will go through all your flock today, setting apart from the lambs every speckled and marked animal and every black animal, and from the goats every marked and speckled animal: this will be my wages.
30.33: My integrity will testify for me tomorrow, when you come to acknowledge my wages. Any goat that is not speckled and marked, or black among the lambs, will be considered theft by me.
30.34: Laban said: Well, let it be according to your word.
30.35: And that same day he set apart the striped and marked male goats, all the speckled and marked female goats, all those that had white, and all the black lambs, and he put them in the hands of his sons.
30.36: Then he put three days' journey between himself and Jacob. And Jacob tended the rest of Laban's flock.
30.37: Jacob took green sticks of poplar, almond, and plane tree, and peeled white strips from them, revealing the white that was on the chopsticks.
30.38: Then he placed the peeled sticks opposite the sheep in the ditches, in the watering troughs where the sheep came to drink, and they went into heat when they came to drink.
30.39: And the ewes, coming into heat in front of the sticks, produced striped, spotted and marked offspring.
30.40: Jacob set aside the lambs, and he turned the face of the flock toward the striped and black animals in Laban's flock. He thus formed his own flocks, which he did not add to Laban's flock.
30.41: Furthermore, It was when the strongest ewes came into heat that Jacob placed the sticks in the watering troughs before the sheep's eyes, so that they would come into heat near the sticks.
30.42: When the sheep were weak, he did not put them out, so the weak lambs were for Laban, and the strong ones for Jacob.
30.43: This man thus became extremely rich; he had many flocks, female and male servants, camels and donkeys.

31.1: Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, who said: Jacob took everything that belonged to our father, and it is with our father's wealth that he has made himself all this wealth.
31.2: Jacob noticed Also Laban's face, and behold, he was no longer toward him as before.
31.3: And the Lord said to Jacob, “Go back to the land of your fathers and to the place of your birth, and I will be with you.
31.4: Then Jacob sent word to Rachel and Leah to come to him in the fields, where he was grazing his flock.
31.5: He said to them, “I see that your father’s face toward me is no longer the same as before, yet the God of my father has been with me.”.
31.6: You yourselves know that I served your father with all my might,
31.7: And your father deceived me, changing my salary ten times, but God did not allow him to harm me.
31.8: When he said, “The speckled animals will be your wages,” all the sheep bore speckled lambs. And if he said, “The striped animals will be your wages,” all the sheep bore striped lambs.
31.9: So God took your father's livestock and gave it to me.
31.10: At the time when the ewes go into heat, I raised my eyes and saw in a dream that the rams that were covering the ewes were striped, speckled and marked.
31.11: And an angel of God said to me in a dream: Jacob. I answered: Here I am.
31.12: And he said: Lift up your eyes and look: all the rams that are covering the ewes are striped, speckled, and marked, for I have seen all that Laban has done to you.
31.13: I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a monument, where you made a vow to me. Now get up, leave this land and return to the land of your birth.
31.14: Rachel and Leah answered, saying, “Do we still have a share and an inheritance in our father’s house?”
31.15: Are we not regarded by him as foreigners, since he sold us and is consuming our money?
31.16: Moreover, all the good that God took away from our father, we and our children possess. So now do what God has commanded you.
31.17: Jacob got up and had his children and wives mount the camels.
31.18: He took all his flock and all the possessions he had acquired, the flock he owned, which he had acquired in Paddan-Aram, and he went to Isaac, his father, in the land of Canaan.
31.19: While Laban was shearing his sheep, Rachel stole her father's idols.
31.20: And Jacob deceived Laban the Aramean by not informing him of his flight.
31.21: He fled, he and all that belonged to him, and having risen, he crossed the river and headed towards the mountain of Gilead.
31.22: On the third day, Laban was told that Jacob had fled.
31.23: He took his brothers with him and pursued him for seven days' journey, until he caught up with him at the mountain of Gilead.
31.24: And God came in a dream by night to Laban the Aramean, and said to him, “Beware of…” Nothing to tell Jacob, neither good nor bad.
31.25: So Laban reached Jacob. Jacob had pitched his tent on the mountain, and Laban had Also erected his own, with his brothers, on the mountain of Gilead.
31.26: Laban said to Jacob, "What have you done? You have deceived my mind and taken my daughters away like captives taken by the sword?"
31.27: Why did you secretly flee and deceive me, instead of warning me, when I would have let you go in the joy of singing, to the sound of the tambourine and the harp?
31.28: You wouldn't let me kiss my sons and daughters. You really acted foolishly.
31.29: My hand is strong enough to harm you, but the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying: Beware of Nothing to tell Jacob, neither good nor bad.
31.30: And now you've left because you longed for your father's house, but, Why did you steal my gods?
31.31: Jacob answered and said to Laban: It was because I was afraid, thinking that perhaps you would take your daughters from me.
31.32: As for the one in whose possession you find your gods, he will not live. In the presence of our brothers, acknowledge what belongs to you with me and take it. Now Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them.
31.33: Laban went into Jacob's tent, into Leah's tent, and into the tents of the two maids, but found nothing. Then he left Leah's tent and went into Rachel's tent.
31.34: Rachel had taken the idols, put them in the camel's saddle, and sat on them. Laban searched the entire tent, but found nothing.
31.35: And Rachel said to her father, “Do not let my lord be angry that I cannot rise before you, for I am experiencing what is customary for women.” He searched, but did not find the idols.
31.36: Jacob became angry and reproached Laban, and Jacob spoke up and said to Laban, “What is my crime, what is my fault, that you are pursuing me relentlessly?”
31.37: When you searched through all my belongings, what did you find of your own household goods? Bring it up here before my brothers and your brothers, and let them judge between the two of us.
31.38: For twenty years I have been with you, your ewes and goats have not miscarried, and I have not eaten the rams of your flock.
31.39: What was torn by wild beasts , I didn't bring it back to you; I was the one who suffered the loss. You were demanding what had been stolen during the day and what had been stolen at night.
31.40: I was consumed by the heat during the day, and by the cold at night, and sleep fled from my eyes.
31.41: I have been in your house for twenty years, I have served you for fourteen years for your two daughters and six years for your livestock, and you have changed my wages ten times.
31.42: If the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the Terror of Isaac had not been with me, you would have let me go empty-handed by now. God has seen my suffering and the labor of my hands, and tonight he has judged between us .
31.43: Laban answered and said to Jacob, “These daughters are my daughters, these children are my children, these flocks are my flocks, and everything you see is mine. What should I do today with my daughters, with them, and with the sons they have borne?”
31.44: Now then, come, let us make a covenant, you and I, and let there be a witness between you and me.
31.45: Jacob took a stone and set it up as a monument.
31.46: And Jacob said to his brothers, “Gather stones.” So they took stones and in They made a heap, and they ate there on the heap.
31.47: Laban called him Jegar-Sahadutha, and Jacob called him Gilead.
31.48: And Laban said, “This heap is a witness between you and me today.” That is why it was named Gilead.,
31.49: and also that of Mizpah because Laban He said: May the Lord watch over you and me when we are separated from each other.
31.50: If you mistreat my daughters and if you take other women alongside my daughters, this is not not a man Who will be with us, but, beware, It is God Who will be a witness between you and me.
31.51: Laban said Again To Jacob: Here is this heap and here is the monument that I have set up between me and you.
31.52: This heap is witness and this monument is witness that I will not advance toward you beyond this heap, and that you will not advance toward me beyond this heap and this monument, to do harm.
31.53: May the God of Abraham, the God of Nahor, and the God of their fathers judge between us. And Jacob swore by the fear of Isaac.
31.54: And Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain, and he invited his brothers to the meal. So they ate and spent the night on the mountain.

32.1: Laban got up early in the morning, kissed his sons and daughters and blessed them, then left to return to his house.
32.2: Jacob continued on his way, and angels of God met him.
32.3: When he saw them, he said: This is the camp of God, and he named that place Mahanaim.
32.4: Jacob sent messengers ahead of him to Esau, his brother, in the land of Seir, in the countryside of Edom.
32.5: He gave them this order: This is what you are to say to my lord Esau: This is what your servant Jacob says: I have been staying with Laban and have remained there until now,
32.6: I have oxen and donkeys, sheep, male and female servants, and I am informing my lord about them, to find favor in your eyes.
32.7: The messengers returned to Jacob, saying, "We went to your brother Esau, and he is coming to meet you with four hundred men.".
32.8: Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed. He divided the people who were with him, the sheep, the cattle, and the camels, into two camps.,
32.9: And he said: If Esau encounters one of the camps and attacks it, the camp that remains may be saved.
32.10: Jacob said: God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, the Lord, who said to me, “Go back to your country and to the place of your birth and I will do you good,”
32.11: I am too small for all the grace and faithfulness you have shown your servant, for I crossed this Jordan with my staff, and now I have become two camps.
32.12: Deliver me, I pray, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau, for I fear that he will come and strike me, the mother with the children.
32.13: And you have said: I will do you good and I will make your descendants like the sand of the sea, so numerous that they cannot be counted.
32.14: Jacob spent the night there. And he took what he had on hand, to TO DO a present to Esau, his brother:
32.15: two hundred goats and twenty billy goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams,
32.16: thirty camels that were nursing, with their young, forty cows and ten bulls, twenty female donkeys and ten foals.
32.17: He THE he gave each flock to his servants, separating them, and said to them, “Go on ahead of me, and leave a space between each flock.”.
32.18: And he gave the first man an order: When Esau, my brother, finds you and asks you, “Whose are you? Where are you going? Whose flock do you belong to?” who will in front of you?
32.19: You will answer: To your servant Jacob, it is a present that he sends to my lord Esau, and behold, he himself comes after us.
32.20: He gave the same order to the second, the third, and all those who followed the flocks, saying: “Speak to Esau this when you meet him,
32.21: You will say: Behold, your servant Jacob is also coming behind us. For he thought: I will appease him with this present that goes before me, and afterward I shall see his face, perhaps he will receive me favorably.
32.22: The present passed before him, and he stayed that night in the camp.
32.23: He got up that same night and, having taken his two wives, his two maids and his eleven children, he crossed the ford of the Jaboc.
32.24: He took them and helped them cross the torrent; he also helped them cross what belonged to him.
32.25: Jacob was left alone, and a man wrestled with him until daybreak.
32.26: Seeing that he could not overcome him, he touched him on the hip joint, and Jacob's hip joint was dislocated as he wrestled with him.
32.27: And he said to Jacob Let me go, for the dawn is breaking. Jacob replied, I will not let you go unless you bless me.
32.28: He said to him, "What is your name?" He replied, "Jacob.".
32.29: And he said, “Your name will no longer be Jacob, but Israel, because you have fought alongside God and…” with men, and you prevailed.
32.30: Jacob asked him, saying, “Please tell me your name.” He said, “Why do you ask what my name is?” And he blessed him there.
32.31: Jacob named the place Phanuel, because, he said, I saw God face to face, and my life was spared.
32.32: And the sun rose when it had passed Phanuel, but it was limping because of its hip.
32.33: That is why the children of Israel do not eat to this day the large sinew that is at the hip joint, because God touched Jacob’s hip joint at the large sinew.

33.1: Jacob lifted his eyes and looked, and behold, Esau was coming, having with He had four hundred men. Having distributed the children in groups with Leah, with Rachel, and with the two maids,
33.2: He placed the maids with their children at the front, then Leah with her children, and finally Rachel with Joseph.
33.3: He himself passed before them, and he bowed down to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother Esau.
33.4: Esau ran to meet her, embraced her, threw himself on her neck and kissed her, and they wept.
33.5: Then, looking up, Esau saw the women and children, and he said, “Who are these with you?” Jacob answered, “They are the children God has given to your servant.”.
33.6: The maids approached, they and their children, and prostrated themselves.
33.7: Leah and her children also approached and bowed down; then Joseph and Rachel approached and bowed down.
33.8: And Esau said: What do you want to do with all these people I've met? And Jacob He said: It is to find favor in the eyes of my lord.
33.9: Esau said, “I have plenty, my brother; keep what is yours.”.
33.10: And Jacob said, “No, I beg you, if I have found favor in your eyes, accept my present from my hand, for it is for this reason that I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me favorably.”.
33.11: Therefore accept my offering which has been brought to you, for God has shown me favor and I lack nothing. He urged him so strongly that Esau accepted it.
33.12: Esau said, “Let us go, let us set out on our journey, I will go before you.”.
33.13: Jacob replied: My lord knows that children are delicate, and that I am burdened with sheep and cows that are nursing; if they were pressed for even one day, the whole flock would perish.
33.14: Let my lord go ahead of his servant, and I will follow gently, at the pace of the flock that goes before me, and at the pace of the children, until I arrive at my lord's house in Seir.
33.15: Esau said, “At least let me leave with you.” a part people who are with me. Jacob replied: Why is that? That I may find favor in the eyes of my lord.
33.16: And Esau returned that day to Seir.
33.17: Jacob went to Socoth and built himself a house. He also made shelters for his flocks, and that is why the place was called Socoth.
33.18: On his return from Paddan-Aram, Jacob arrived safely at the city of Shechem in the land of Canaan, and he camped before the city.
33.19: He bought from the sons of Hemor, the father of Shechem, for one hundred pieces of land, the piece of ground where he had pitched his tent,
33.20: He erected an altar there and called it El-Elohe-Israel.

34.1: Dina, the daughter whom Leah had borne to Jacob, went out to see the women of the land.
34.2: Shechem, son of Hemor the Hivite, prince of the land, having seen her, seized her, lay with her and violated her.
34.3: His soul was drawn to Dinah, daughter of Jacob; he loved the young girl and spoke to her heart.
34.4: And Shechem said to Hemor, his father, “Take this young woman for me as a wife.”.
34.5: Now Jacob learned that he had outraged Dinah, his daughter, but, as his sons were in the fields with his flock, Jacob kept silent until their return.
34.6: Hemor, Shechem's father, went out to Jacob to speak to him.
34.7: Now the sons of Jacob had returned from the fields when they heard of it, and these men were outraged and became very angry, because Shechem had committed an outrage against Israel by lying with Jacob’s daughter, which should not have been done.
34.8: Hemor spoke to them thus: The soul of Shechem, my son, has become attached to your daughter, give her to him as a wife, I beg you.
34.9: If you ally yourself with us, you will give us your daughters, and you will take ours for yourselves.
34.10: You will live in our country, and the country will be at your disposal, for you to settle in, trade in, and acquire property.
34.11: Shechem said to Dinah's father and brothers, "Let me find favor in your eyes, and I will give you what you ask for.".
34.12: Demand a high purchase price and large gifts from me, and whatever you tell me, I will give it, but give me the young girl as my wife.
34.13: Jacob's sons answered and spoke cunningly to Shechem and to Hamor, his father, because Shechem had dishonored Dinah, their sister,
34.14: They said to them, "We cannot do this, to give our sister to an uncircumcised man, for that would be a disgrace to us.".
34.15: We will only consent to your desire on the condition that you become like us, and that every male among you is circumcised.
34.16: So we will give you our daughters, and we will take your daughters for ourselves, we will live with you and become one people.
34.17: But if you refuse to listen to us and circumcise yourselves, we will take our daughter back and leave.
34.18: Their words pleased Hamor and Shechem, Hamor's sons,
34.19: And the young man did not delay in doing the thing, for he was in love with Jacob's daughter, and he was the most respected man in his father's house.
34.20: Hemor and Shechem, his son, surrendered SO at the city gate, and they spoke to the men of their city, saying:
34.21: These people are peaceful men among us; let them settle in the land and trade, for the land is large enough for them on both sides. We will take their daughters as wives, and we will give them our daughters.
34.22: But these men will only agree to live with us, to become one people, on one condition: that every male among us be circumcised, as they themselves are circumcised.
34.23: Will not their herds, their possessions, and all their pack animals be ours? Only, let us access their desire and that they settle among us.
34.24: All those who went out of the city gate listened to Hemor and Shechem, his son, and every male was circumcised, every man going out of the city gate.
34.25: On the third day, when they were sick, two of Jacob’s sons, Simeon and Levi, Dinah’s brothers, each took his sword, marched fearlessly on the city, and killed all the males.
34.26: They passed Also with the edge of the sword Hemor and Shechem, his son, and, having taken Dinah from the house of Shechem, they went out.
34.27: Jacob's sons attacked the slain and plundered the city, because their sister had been dishonored.
34.28: They took their sheep, their oxen and their donkeys, what was in the city and what was in the fields.
34.29: They took as plunder all their possessions, their children and their wives, and everything that was in the houses.
34.30: Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “You have troubled me by making me odious to the inhabitants of the land, to the Canaanites and the Perizzites. I have only a few people with me; they will gather together against me and kill me, and I and my household will be destroyed.”.
34.31: They replied: Will our sister be treated like a prostitute?

35.1: God said to Jacob, “Arise, go up to Bethel and dwell there, and erect there an altar to the God who appeared to you when you were fleeing from Esau, your brother.”.
35.2: Jacob said to his family and to all who were with him: Put away the foreign gods that are among you, purify yourselves and change your clothes.
35.3: We shall arise and go up to Bethel, and there I will set up an altar to the God who answered me in the day of my distress, and who has been with me on the journey that I have made.
35.4: And they gave Jacob all the foreign gods that were in their possession and the earrings that were in their ears, and Jacob buried them under the terebinth tree that is in Shechem.
35.5: They departed, and the terror of God spread over the surrounding cities, and the sons of Jacob were not pursued.
35.6: Jacob, with all the people who were with him, arrived at Luz, in the land of Canaan: that is Bethel.
35.7: He built an altar there, and he called the place El-Bethel, because it was there that God had appeared to him when he was fleeing from his brother.
35.8: SO Deborah, Rebecca's nurse, died, and she was buried below Bethel, at the foot of the oak tree which was given the name Oak of Weeping.
35.9: God appeared again to Jacob, after his return from Paddan-Aram, and blessed him.
35.10: God said to him, “Your name is Jacob, but you will no longer be called Jacob, but Israel.” And he named him Israel.
35.11: God said to him, “I am God Almighty. Be fruitful and multiply, and a nation and a community of nations will come from you, and kings will come from your loins.”.
35.12: The land that I gave to Abraham and Isaac, I will give to you, and I will give This country to your posterity after you.
35.13: And God went up from him to the place where he had spoken to him.
35.14: And in the place where he had spoken to him, Jacob erected a stone monument, on which he poured a libation and oil.
35.15: He named the place where God had spoken to him Bethel.
35.16: They left Bethel. There was still some distance before they reached Ephrath when Rachel gave birth, and her delivery was difficult.
35.17: During the pains of childbirth, the midwife told her: Do not be afraid, for it is yet another son that you will have.
35.18: As her soul was departing, for she was dying, she named him Benoni, but his father called him Benjamin.
35.19: Rachel died, and she was buried on the road to Ephrathah, which is Bethlehem.
35.20: Jacob erected a monument over her grave; this is the monument of Rachel's Tomb, which still stands today.
35.21: Israel departed, and pitched their tent beyond Migdal-Eder.
35.22: While Israel was living in that land, Reuben came and lay with Balaam, his father's concubine, and Israel heard of it. Jacob's sons were twelve in number.
35.23: Leah's sons: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.
35.25: Sons of Balaam, Rachel's servant: Dan and Naphtali.
35.26: The sons of Zelpah, Leah's servant: Gad and Asher. These were the sons of Jacob, who were born to him in Paddan-Aram.
35.27: Jacob arrived at Mamre, at Kiriath-Arbe, which is Hebron, where Abraham and Isaac had stayed.
35.28: Isaac lived for one hundred and eighty years.
35.29: Isaac breathed his last and died, and was gathered to his people, old and full of days. Esau and Jacob, his sons, buried him.

36.1: This is the story of Esau, who is Edom.
36.2: Esau took wives from among the daughters of Canaan: Adah, daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Oolibabah, daughter of Anah, daughter of Shebeon the Hivite,
36.3: and Basemath, daughter of Ishmael, sister of Nabaioth.
36.4: Ada bore Eliphaz to Esau, Basemath bore Rahuel,
36.5: And Oolibaba fathered Jehus, Ihelon, and Korah. These were the sons of Esau, who were born to him in the land of Canaan.
36.6: Esau took his wives, his sons and daughters, and all the members of his household, his flocks, all his livestock, and all the possessions he had acquired in the land of Canaan, and he went to a other country, far from Jacob, his brother.
36.7: Because their possessions were too considerable for them to remain together, and the country where they stayed could not suffice for them because of their flocks.
36.8: Esau settled in the mountains of Seir; Esau is Edom.
36.9: This is the account of the descendants of Esau, the father of Edom, in the mountains of Seir.
36.10: These are the names of Esau's sons: Eliphaz, son of Adah, Esau's wife; Rauel, son of Basemath, Esau's wife.
36.11: The sons of Eliphaz were: Teman, Omar, Zipporah, Gatham, and Zenez.
36.12: Thamna was a concubine of Eliphaz, son of Esau, and she bore Amalek to Eliphaz. These were the sons of Adah, Esau's wife.
36.13: These are the sons of Rahuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Meza. These are the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.
36.14: These are the sons of Oolibabah, daughter of Anah, daughter of Shebeon, wife of Esau: she bore to Esau Jehus, Jehlon and Korah.
36.15: Here are the leaders tribes originating from The sons of Esau. The sons of Eliphaz, Esau's firstborn: Chief Teman, Chief Omar, Chief Zippor, Chief Kenez,
36.16: Chief Kore, Chief Gatham, Chief Amalek. These are the chiefs from from Eliphaz, in the land of Edom, these are the sons of Ada.
36.17: The sons of Rauel, son of Esau: Chief Nahath, Chief Zara, Chief Shammah, and Chief Meza. These are the chiefs from from Rahuel, in the land of Edom, these are the sons of Basemath, wife of Esau.
36.18: The sons of Oolibabah, Esau's wife: Chief Jehus, Chief Jelon, and Chief Korah. These were the chiefs from of Oolibaba, daughter of Ana and wife of Esau.
36.19: These are the sons of Esau, and these are their leaders; this is Edom.
36.20: These are the sons of Seir the Horite, who lived in the land: Lotan, Zobal, Shebeon, Anah,
36.21: Dison, Eser and Disan. These are the leaders of the Horites, sons of Seir, in the land of Edom.
36.22: Lotan's sons were Hori and Heman, and Tammah was Lotan's sister.
36.23: These are the sons of Sobal: Alvan, Manahat, Ebal, Sepho, and Onam.
36.24: These are the sons of Shebeon: Ajah and Anah. It was this Anah who found the hot springs in the desert, while tending the donkeys of Shebeon, his father.
36.25: These are Ana's children: Dison and Oolibama, Ana's daughter.
36.26: These are the sons of Dison: Hamdan, Eseban, Jethram, and Haran.
36.27: These are the sons of Eser: Balan, Zavan, and Achan.
36.28: These are the sons of Disan: Hus and Aram.
36.29: These are the leaders of the Horreans: Chief Lotan, Chief Sobal, Chief Sebeon, Chief Ana,
36.30: Chief Dison, Chief Eser, Chief Disan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, each of their chiefs in the land of Seir.
36.31: These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before a king reigned over the children of Israel:
36.32: Bela, son of Beor, reigned in Edom, and the name of his city was Denaba.
36.33: Bela died, and Jobab, son of Zerah, of Bozrah, reigned in his place.
36.34: Jobab died, and Husam of the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.
36.35: Husam died, and in his place reigned Adad, son of Badad, who defeated Midian in the fields of Moab; the name of his city was Avith.
36.36: Hadad died, and in his place reigned Semla of Masreca.
36.37: Shemlah died, and in his place Saul of Rohoboth on the river reigned.
36.38: Saul died, and Balanan son of Achor reigned in his place.
36.39: Balanan, son of Achor, died, and Hadar reigned in his place; the name of his city was Phaul, and the name of his wife Meabel, daughter of Matred, daughter of Mezaab.
36.40: Here are the names of the leaders from of Esau, according to their tribes, their territories, and their names: Chief Tammah, Chief Alva, Chief Jetheth,
36.41: Chief Oolibaba, Chief Ela, Chief Phinon,
36.42: Chief Cénez, Chief Théman, Chief Mabsar,
36.43: Chief Magdiel, Chief Hiram. These are the chiefs of Edom, according to their dwellings in the land they occupy. This is Esau, the father of Edom.

37.1: Jacob settled in the land where his father had stayed in the land of Canaan.
37.2: This is the story of Jacob. Joseph, seventeen years old, was tending sheep with his brothers. Since he was still young, he was with the sons of Balaam and the sons of Zelpah, his father's wives, and Joseph brought back bad rumors to their father. who were running on their account.
37.3: Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his other sons, because he was a son of his old age, and he made him a long robe.
37.4: His brothers, seeing that their father loved him more than all of them, came to hate him, and they could no longer speak to him amicably.
37.5: Joseph had a dream, and he told it to his brothers, who hated him even more.
37.6: He said to them: Listen, I beg you, to the dream I had:
37.7: We were binding sheaves in the middle of the fields, and behold, my sheaf rose up and stood upright, and your sheaves surrounded it and bowed down before it.
37.8: His brothers said to him, “Will you reign over us? Will you rule over us?” And they hated him even more for his dreams and his words.
37.9: He had yet another dream, which he told to his brothers. He said: I had another dream: the sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me.
37.10: He told it to his father and brothers, and his father rebuked him, saying, “What is this dream you had? Must we, your mother, and your brothers come and bow down to the ground before you?”
37.11: And his brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the thing in his heart .
37.12: Joseph's brothers went to herd their father's flocks in Shechem.
37.13: And Israel said to Joseph, “Are not your brothers tending the flock at Shechem? Come, I will send you to them.” He answered, “Here I am.”.
37.14: And Israel said to him, “Go now and see if your brothers are well and if the flock is in good condition, and bring me word again.” So he sent him from the valley of Hebron, and Joseph went to Shechem.
37.15: A man, having met him wandering in the countryside, questioned him, saying: What are you looking for?
37.16: He replied, “I am looking for my brothers; please tell me where they are grazing.” their herd .
37.17: And the man said, “They have left here, for I heard them say, ‘Let us go to Dothain.’” Joseph went after his brothers and found them at Dothain.
37.18: They saw him from afar and, before he came near them, they plotted to kill him.
37.19: They said to each other: Here is the man of dreams, it is he who is coming.
37.20: Come then, let us kill him and throw him into one of the cisterns, and we will say that a ferocious beast devoured him, we will see what will become of his dreams.
37.21: Reuben heard and rescued him from their hands. He said, “Let’s not strike him.” to death .
37.22: Reuben said to them, “Do not shed blood; throw it into this cistern in the desert, and do not lay a hand on him.” His intention was to rescue him from their hands and bring him back to his father.
37.23: When Joseph arrived in front of his brothers, they stripped him of his robe, the long robe he was wearing,
37.24: And, having taken it, they threw it into the cistern. This cistern was empty: there was no water in it.
37.25: Then they sat down to eat. Lifting their eyes, they saw, and behold, a caravan of Ishmaelites was coming from Gilead, their camels were loaded with astragalus, balm and ladanum, which they were transporting to Egypt.
37.26: Then Judah said to his brothers, “What will we gain if we kill our brother and conceal his blood?”
37.27: Let us go and sell him to the Ishmaelites, but let us not lay a hand on him, for he is our brother, our flesh. His brothers listened to him.
37.28: And when the Midianite merchants passed by, they pulled Joseph out of the cistern and sold him for twenty pieces of silver to the Ishmaelites, who took him to Egypt.
37.29: Reuben returned to the cistern, and behold, Joseph was no longer in the cistern.
37.30: He tore his clothes and, returning to his brothers, said: The child n ‘'y is no more, and where will I go?
37.31: They took SO Joseph's robe and, having killed a goat, they dipped the robe in the blood.
37.32: And they sent the long robe to their father, saying, "This is what we have found; see if this is your son's robe or not.".
37.33: Jacob recognized her and said: It is My son's robe. A ferocious beast devoured it. Joseph was torn to pieces.
37.34: And he tore his clothes, put sackcloth on his loins, and mourned for his son for a long time.
37.35: All his sons and daughters came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted, saying, “I will go down to my son in mourning to the realm of the dead.” And his father wept for him.
37.36: The Midianites sold him in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, the chief of the guards.

38.1: At that time Judah, walking away of his brothers, went down and came to a man from Odollam named Hira.
38.2: There Judah saw the daughter of a Canaanite named Shuh, and he took her for women and went towards her.
38.3: She conceived and gave birth to a son, and he named him Her.
38.4: She conceived again and gave birth to a son, and she named him Onan.
38.5: She conceived again and gave birth to a son, and she named him Selah; Judah was in Achzib when she gave birth to him.
38.6: Judah took a wife named Tamar for Her, his firstborn.
38.7: Her, the firstborn of Judah, was wicked in the sight of the Lord, and the Lord put him to death.
38.8: Then Judah said to Onan, “Go in to your brother’s wife, fulfill your duty as a brother-in-law, and raise up offspring for your brother.”.
38.9: But Onan knew that this offspring would not be his, and when he went to his brother's wife, he defiled himself on the ground so as not to give offspring to his brother.
38.10: His actions displeased the Lord, who also put him to death.
38.11: And Judah said to Tamar, his daughter-in-law, “Remain as a widow in your father’s house until my son Sela grows up.” For he thought, “He must not die like his brothers.” So Tamar went and remained in her father’s house.
38.12: After many days, the daughter of Shuh, Judah's wife, died. When Judah had finished his mourning, he and his friend Hirah the Odollamite went up to those who were shearing his sheep at Thamna.
38.13: They informed Thamar, saying: Here is your father-in-law going up to Thamna to shear his sheep.
38.14: So she took off her widow's clothes, covered herself with a veil, and, thus wrapped, she sat at the entrance of Enaim, on the road to Thamna, for she saw that Sela had grown up and that she was not given to him as a wife.
38.15: Judah, having seen her, took her to be a woman of ill repute, for she had covered her face.
38.16: He went his own way, towards the road, and said, “Let me come to you.” For he did not know that she was his daughter-in-law. She said, “What will you give me to come to me?”
38.17: He replied: I will send you a kid from the flock. She said: On the condition that you give me a pledge until you send it.
38.18: He said, "What pledge shall I give you?" She said, "Your ring, your cord, and the staff that is in your hand." He gave them to her and went in to her, and she conceived by him.
38.19: Then she got up, went away, and took off her veil and put on her widow's clothes.
38.20: Judah sent the kid by his friend, the Odollamite, to retrieve the pledge from the woman's hand, but he did not find her.
38.21: He questioned the people of the place, saying, "Where is that prostitute who stood by the roadside in Enaim?" They answered, "There was no prostitute here.".
38.22: So he returned to Judah and said, “I did not find her, and even the people of the place said, ‘There has been no prostitute here.’”.
38.23: Judah said: Let her keep his pledge , We mustn't be mocked. Here, I have sent the kid promised , And you didn't find it.
38.24: About three months later, Judah was told, “Tamar, your daughter-in-law, has played the harlot, and now she is pregnant as a result of her prostitution.” Judah said, “Bring her out and let her be burned.”.
38.25: As they were leading her outside, she sent word to her father-in-law: "It is by the man to whom these objects belong that I am pregnant. Look closely," she added, "at whose ring, cord, and staff this is.".
38.26: Judah recognized them and said, "She is more righteous than I, since I did not give her to my son Shelah." And he did not have relations with her again.
38.27: When the time came for her to give birth, behold, there were two twins in her womb.
38.28: During the delivery, one of them stretched out his hand, the midwife took it and tied a scarlet thread to it, saying: This is the one who came out first.
38.29: But he withdrew his hand, and behold, his brother came out. "What a breach you have made!" said the midwife. "The breach is upon you!" And he was named Peres.
38.30: Then came out his brother, who had the scarlet thread in his hand, and he was named Zara.

39.1: Joseph was taken to Egypt, and Potiphar, an Egyptian officer of Pharaoh and commander of the guards, bought him from the Ishmaelites who had brought him there.
39.2: The Lord was with Joseph, who prospered in all things, and he lived in the house of his master, the Egyptian.
39.3: His master saw that the Lord was with him and that the Lord made everything he did successful.
39.4: Joseph found SO thanks to her eyes, he was employed in her service., his master He placed him in charge of his house and gave him back everything he had.
39.5: As soon as he had put him over his house and over all that he had, the Lord blessed the house of the Egyptian because of Joseph, and the blessing of the Lord was on all that he had, both in the house and in the fields.
39.6: And he left everything he had in Joseph's hands, no longer inquiring about anything except the food he ate. Now Joseph was handsome in body and appearance.
39.7: After these things, his master's wife cast her eyes on Joseph and him said: Sleep with me.
39.8: He refused and said to his master's wife, "Look, my master does not inquire with me about anything in the house, and he has put everything he has into my hands.".
39.9: He is not greater than I in this house, and he has forbidden me nothing except you, because you are his wife. How could I do such a great evil and sin against God?
39.10: Whatever in Although she spoke to Joseph every day, he did not consent to sleep with her or be with her.
39.11: One day, when he had entered the house to do his chores, and none of the household staff were present,
39.12: She grabbed him by his garment, saying, "Lie with me." But he left his garment in her hand and ran outside.
39.13: When she saw that he had left her garment in her hand and had run outside,
39.14: She called the people of her household and spoke to them, saying, “Look, he has brought us a Hebrew to have sex with us. This man came to me to lie with me, and I called out loudly.”.
39.15: And when he heard me raise my voice and shout, he left his garment beside me and ran outside.
39.16: Then she laid Joseph's garment beside her until his master returned home.
39.17: And she spoke to him according to these words, saying: The Hebrew servant whom you brought to us came to me to frolic with me.
39.18: And when I raised my voice and shouted, he left his garment beside me and ran outside.
39.19: When the master of Joseph had heard the words of his wife, who spoke to him in these terms: This is what your servant has done to me, and his anger was kindled.
39.20: He took Joseph and put him in prison, which was the place where the king's prisoners were held. And he was there in prison.
39.21: The Lord was with Joseph, he extended his kindness to him, and gave him favor in the eyes of the prison warden.
39.22: And the prison warden placed all the prisoners in the prison under his supervision, and everything that was done there was done by him.
39.23: The prison warden paid no attention to any of that Joseph had in his hands, because the Lord was with him, and the Lord made everything he did succeed,

40.1: After these things, it happened that the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt offended their master, the king of Egypt.
40.2: Pharaoh was angry with his two officers, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker,
40.3: and he had them locked up in the house of the chief of the guards, in the prison, in the place where Joseph was confined.
40.4: The chief of the guards placed Joseph among them, and he served them, and they were in prison for a while.
40.5: The cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, who were locked in the prison, both had a dream on the same night, each his own, with a different meaning.
40.6: Joseph, having come to them in the morning, looked at them, and behold, they were sad.
40.7: He questioned SO Pharaoh's officers who were with him in prison, in his master's house, and said to them, "Why do you look so sad today?"
40.8: They told him, "We had a dream, and there is no one there." here to explain it. And Joseph said to them, “Do not interpretations belong to God? Please tell me, your dream .
40.9: The chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream, saying: In my dream, behold, there was a vine in front of me.,
40.10: And this vine had three branches, it sprouted buds, the flower appeared, and its clusters produced ripe grapes.
40.11: Pharaoh's cup was in my hand, I took some grapes, squeezed the juice into Pharaoh's cup, and placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand.
40.12: Joseph said to him: Here is the interpretation: the three branches are three days.
40.13: In three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your office, and you will place Pharaoh's cup in his hand, according your first office, when you were his cupbearer.
40.14: If you remember me when happiness is restored to you, and if you deign to show me kindness, remind me of me. souvenir Pharaoh, and get me out of this house.
40.15: For it was by rapture that I was taken from the land of the Hebrews, and here I have done nothing to deserve being put in this prison.
40.16: The chief baker, seeing that Joseph had given a favorable interpretation, said to him: I too, in my dream, was seeing three baskets of white bread on my head.
40.17: In the basket above were all kinds of pastries for Pharaoh, and the birds ate them from the basket that was on my head.
40.18: Joseph replied: Here is the interpretation: the three baskets are three days.
40.19: In three days Pharaoh will lift your head from you and hang you on a tree, and the birds will devour your flesh from you.
40.20: On the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, he gave a feast for all his servants, and he lifted up the head of the chief cupbearer and the head of the chief baker:
40.21: He reinstated the chief cupbearer in his office, and the latter placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand.,
40.22: and he hanged the chief baker, according to the interpretation that Joseph had given them.
40.23: But the chief cupbearer did not mention Joseph, and forgot about him.

41.1: Two years having passed, Pharaoh had a dream. Behold, he was standing by the river,
41.2: And behold, seven cows, beautiful to behold and fat with flesh, came up from the river, and they began to graze in the greenery.
41.3: And behold, after them came up from the river seven other cows, ugly to behold and thin of flesh, and they came and stood beside the cows who were on the riverbank.
41.4: And the ugly, thin cows devoured the seven beautiful, fat cows. Then Pharaoh awoke.
41.5: He fell asleep again, and had a second dream. And behold, seven ears of grain were growing from the same stalk, plump and beautiful.
41.6: And seven thin ears of corn, scorched by the east wind, grew after those.
41.7: And the thin ears of grain swallowed the seven plump, full ears. Then Pharaoh awoke. And there it was, it was a dream.
41.8: In the morning, Pharaoh was troubled in mind, and he summoned all the scribes and wise men of Egypt. He told them his dreams, but no one could interpret them for Pharaoh.
41.9: Then the chief cupbearer, speaking up, said to Pharaoh: I will now recall my sins.
41.10: Pharaoh was angry with his servants, and he put me and the chief baker in prison in the house of the captain of the guard.
41.11: We both had a dream on the same night, he and I, we each dreamed according to the meaning of our own dream.
41.12: There was with us a young Hebrew man, a servant of the captain of the guard. We told him our dreams, and he interpreted them for each of us.,
41.13: And things happened as he had interpreted: Pharaoh reinstated me in my position, and he was hanged.
41.14: Pharaoh sent for Joseph, and he was quickly brought out of prison. He shaved, put on new clothes, and went to Pharaoh.
41.15: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I had a dream that no one can interpret, and I heard it said of you that when you hear a dream, you interpret it.".
41.16: Joseph answered Pharaoh, saying: It is not I, but God who will give Pharaoh a favorable answer.
41.17: Pharaoh said SO To Joseph: In my dream, behold, I was standing on the bank of the river,
41.18: And behold, seven fat cows, well-fed and beautiful in appearance, came up out of the river and began to graze in the greenery.
41.19: And behold, after them came seven other cows, thin, very ugly in appearance and gaunt, I have not seen such ugly ones in all the land of Egypt.
41.20: The thin, ugly cows devoured the first seven cows, those who were fats,
41.21: These entered their bellies, without it appearing that they had entered; their appearance was as ugly as before. And I awoke.
41.22: I live Again in a dream, and behold, seven ears of grain, full and beautiful, were growing on a single stalk.,
41.23: And behold, seven scrawny ears of corn, thin and scorched by the east wind, which grew up after these.
41.24: And the thin ears of grain swallowed up the seven good ears. I told this to the scribes, and no one two Don't explain it to me.
41.25: Joseph said to Pharaoh: Pharaoh's dream is one; God has shown Pharaoh what he is going to do.
41.26: The seven beautiful cows are seven years, and the seven beautiful ears of corn are seven years; it is a single dream.
41.27: The seven scrawny, ugly cows that came up after them represent seven years, and the seven empty ears of grain, scorched by the east wind, represent seven years of famine.
41.28: This is the word I spoke to Pharaoh: God showed Pharaoh what he was going to do.
41.29: Behold, seven years of great abundance are coming throughout the land of Egypt.
41.30: Seven years of famine will follow, and all this abundance in the land of Egypt will be forgotten, and the famine will consume the land.
41.31: The abundance will no longer be noticeable because of the famine that will follow in the country, so great will it be.
41.32: And if the dream was repeated to Pharaoh twice, it is because the matter is decided by God, and God will hasten to execute it.
41.33: Now let Pharaoh find a wise and intelligent man, and let him set him over the land of Egypt.
41.34: Pharaoh should also appoint officials over the land to raise a fifth harvests of the land of Egypt during the seven years of plenty.
41.35: Let them gather all the produce of these good years to come, let them make heaps of grain at Pharaoh's disposal, as provisions in the cities, and let them store them.
41.36: These provisions will be a reserve for the country during the seven years of famine that will come upon the land of Egypt, and the country will not be consumed by famine.
41.37: These words pleased Pharaoh and all his servants.
41.38: And Pharaoh said to his servants, “Can we find a man like this, in whom is the spirit of God?”
41.39: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has shown you all these things, there is no one as intelligent and wise as you.”.
41.40: You will rule my house, and all my people will obey your words; only through the throne will I be greater than you.
41.41: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Behold, I have appointed you over all the land of Egypt.”.
41.42: And Pharaoh took his signet ring from his hand and put it on Joseph's hand, and he dressed him in fine linen garments and put a gold chain around his neck.
41.43: He had him ride on the second of his chariots, and they shouted before him, "Bow down!" So he was established over all the land of Egypt.
41.44: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I am Pharaoh, and without you no one will lift a hand or a foot in all the land of Egypt.”.
41.45: Pharaoh named Joseph Zaphenath-Paneah, and gave him Aseneth, daughter of Potiphar, priest of On, as his wife. And Joseph went to visit the land of Egypt.
41.46: Joseph was thirty years old when he appeared before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, and he left Pharaoh to travel throughout the land of Egypt.
41.47: The land yielded abundantly during the seven years of plenty.
41.48: Joseph gathered all the produce from the seven good years that there were in the land of Egypt, and he made supplies in the cities, depositing in the interior of each city the produce of the surrounding fields.
41.49: Joseph amassed wheat like the sand of the sea, in such great quantity that they stopped counting it, because it was without number.
41.50: Before the year of famine arrived, two sons were born to Joseph, whom Aseneth, daughter of Potiphar, priest of On, bore to him.
41.51: Joseph named his firstborn Manasseh, because, he said, God made me forget all my sorrow and all my father's house.
41.52: He named the second Ephraim, because, he said, God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.
41.53: The seven years of plenty that were in Egypt having come to an end,
41.54: The seven years of famine began to come, just as Joseph had foretold. There was famine in all the lands, while there was bread throughout the land of Egypt.
41.55: Then the whole land of Egypt was also starving, and the people cried out to Pharaoh for to have bread. And Pharaoh said to all the Egyptians, "Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you.".
41.56: Since famine was spreading throughout the land, Joseph opened all the granaries that had been built up there and sold wheat to the Egyptians, and the famine increased in the land of Egypt.
41.57: People from all over the earth came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph, because the famine had become severe throughout the land.

42.1: Jacob, seeing that there was grain in Egypt, said to his sons, "Why are you looking at each other?"
42.2: He said, “Look, I have heard that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy some for us, so that we may live and not die.”.
42.3: Ten of Joseph's brothers went down to Egypt to buy grain.
42.4: But for Benjamin, Joseph's brother, Jacob did not send him with his brothers, because he said to himself, "There is a fear that something bad may happen to him.".
42.5: The children of Israel came SO to buy wheat, along with others who came Also , because the famine was in the land of Canaan.
42.6: Joseph was the leader of the land, and he was the one who sold grain to all the people of the land. When Joseph's brothers arrived, they bowed down before him with their faces to the ground.
42.7: When Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them, but he pretended to be a stranger to them, and spoke rudely to them, saying: Where do you come from? They answered: From the land of Canaan, to buy food.
42.8: Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him.
42.9: Joseph remembered SO dreams he had had about them, and he said to them: You are spies, you have come to discover the weak points of the country.
42.10: They replied, "No, my lord, your servants have come to buy food.".
42.11: We are all sons of the same man, we are honest people, your servants are not spies.
42.12: He told them: Not at all, you have come to identify the weak points of the country.
42.13: They answered, “We, your servants, are twelve brothers, sons of one man, in the land of Canaan. And behold, the youngest is now with our father, and there is one who is no more.”.
42.14: Joseph said to them: It is as I just told you, you are spies.
42.15: In this you will be tested: by the life of Pharaoh you will not get out of here until your younger brother comes.
42.16: Send one of you to fetch your brother, and you remain prisoners. Your words will be Thus tests, and we'll see If the truth is with you, otherwise, by the life of Pharaoh, you are spies.
42.17: And he had them put in prison together for three days.
42.18: On the third day, Joseph said to them, “Do this and you will live: I fear God.”.
